background intentionally vague about details i m in my s i graduated from a bachelor s degree in computer science in the late s which i completed while working in a computer science adjacent field something fairly specific where very few people in the software industry will have experience it had some python coding here and there a little data science kind of stuff but was mostly working in excel and some cots software albeit on very complex models that should have been done in an actual coding language after a few years there i got my first software job as an intermediate software engineer working for a private company on a government contract about months into the job the pandemic hit we managed to work from home but i really didn t get assigned any work i rode that out for a just got hired at a different place doing basically the same thing as that first job i mentioned but this time in more of a software engineering kind of role doing the same work but in python instead control over the input data having to roll back to an earlier version of python during active development because of procurement issues you know standard software engineering problems i ve mostly few years i m not sure that i ll actually miss the coding part of the job though coding is fine i enjoy solving problems and it s a tool i can use to do that but it s never been something i thoroughly enjoy i have personal projects i d like to do courses i d like to take employer is paying for education training etc and i think i ll probably enjoy coding for those more when i m not spending so much time doing it at work x b any advice on how to fake it till i make it books to read things to watch etc would be greatly appreciated anyone with similar experiences who want to weigh in is welcome anyone who wants to confirm that i m a complete imposter who has no right to manage anyone with experience i mean i guess i should know now x b tldr yet another imposter syndrome post i feel like i skipped the entire junior part of my career i m being thrust into managing people who probably know more than me about software development and i feel like a fraud', 'so after being made redundant at the end of february and wanting to make a transition from hardware to software i finally got a job offer and will be starting as a data scientist at a london based startup on monday i thought i d share my experience so my background was as a hardware verification engineer of and half years and i have a masters in electronic engineering and did a few electronic related internships i really liked my job as a verification engineer it was a great team interesting work but if i m honest the salary and progression wasn t as attractive and i had a feeling for a while that i d do better in software so march last year i started a week full stack web development course on coursera exploring javascript react node mongodb alongside a pure javascript course on udemy the full stack course was a great course but by the time i d completed it maybe because the project was fairly boiler plate i wasn t sure backend or frontend development was where i could see myself doing and at that time i figured why not just keep learning so then i started a short udemy course in data science and really felt it was an area i could see myself doing long term i picked up a few more udemy courses strengthening by python learned sql learned tableau and started reading books on machine learning and data science python packages after completing a bunch of the kaggle microcourses that i was really enjoying i found that the content creator had also helped udacity form their nanodegree programmes the month nanodegree looked pretty appealing but expensive they partner with aws and kaggle and give you guided projects and a capstone project so in june i took a free trial for a month a few weeks go by and i m really hating the course the concepts are fine but understanding the aws sagemaker api at the same time as seeing new machine learning concepts is an incredibly dry subject matter and it sapped my motivation up until now i d been doing or so hours of learning a day and this was pushing me to procrastinate and do nothing at the end of the month i d completed most of the modules and learning notwithstanding the capstone project and guided projects and was going to cancel but they hooked me with a pretty good deal so i figured it could be worth having some projects on my portfolio that s when things got really enjoyable the capstone project i chose was a customer segmentation project that involved lots of data cleaning data science unsupervised machine learning and supervised learning i ended up taking six weeks on that project costing another month of subscription it was probably worth it though because the final model i submitted into a kaggle contest ended up coming first place i figured if ever a personal project or mooc course could be taken seriously this would be something worth shouting about at this time it was the end of august so i d officially been out of work months and i was now starting to apply for data science roles and getting absolutely nowhere my partner has a background in design and copyrighting and helped me massively with the presentation of my cv and coverletter but i was hearing nothing despite applying for everything while applying for jobs i continued my learning i managed to get financial aid from coursera to complete there week tensorflow course which i found really underwhelming i d highly recommend a book called deep learning by francois chollet instead i finished the first part of an advanced python deep dive course by fred baptiste on udemy which is absolutely fantastic and i highly recommend to anyone at any level of learning python and i picked up a book of sql practice problems since even though i d learned the syntax i wasn t too confident on my querying as october came round more recruiters were being quite candid with me that without a masters in machine learning or a phd companies at this time wouldn t be interested covid had created a real buyers market for data science and machine learning roles so i started building my software skills more to see if i could get a python developer role i read test driven development by kent beck and worked through the examples in python with pytest i took out a leetcode subscription for a month and was doing a few hours most days i really don t like leetcode i know it gets a lot of praise on here but to be honest i think it s teaching side is poor and found myself using other resources to learn the theory of algorithms and data structures i was getting ok these areas but still finding i d fail a codility coding assessment which was incredibly frustrating given i have and half years experience writing object oriented code in december i focused on learning flask i found it really difficult to motivate myself i think learning web development can be quite difficult because the projects are pretty boiler plate and i was just learning the same sort of functionality and theory of my first course but in a different language and framework also the book i bought on flask was in my view not easy to follow i did however find a great course on udemy that combined flask with a few tools like docker redis cucumber and pytest to build a more complex application and that was going pretty well until i finally lost a lot of general motivation and drive over christmas with the continued and sustained job rejection and feeling overwhelmed by all the different areas i was trying to learn and feeling like i either wasn t giving enough time to any of them or unsure whether any them would actually help get even a foot in the door i d had a few really promising interviews in late november early december but was then getting radio silence which probably contributed to this first week of january comes round new year new me i pour more energy into writing a script of my professional experience up until now and at my previous roles walking through all my technical experience i practice this script with my partner getting the delivery natural and relaxed i start applying to as many roles as i can having spoken with dozens of recruiters and my cv having gone through a number of iterations i have built a good idea of what s made me interesting to companies and what i need to promote and showcase more of for the first half of my job applications i was definitely underselling myself and not appreciating how verification engineering comes across from listening to the pragmatic programmer i realised that a lot of what i had been doing had been software so i started to really tone down any language that might suggest i was making a big transition and outlined my software experience on recruiter calls i would take the time to talk increasingly longer about what verification entailed and tried to position myself as essentially an overlooked diamond in the rough i rebuttled any concerns about not having years of relevant commercial experience by hammering my achievements and abilities and the highlighting the reputation of my last company finally last friday i had a zoom interview with a startup who wanted a python developer with some data science capabilities we talked through my experience they were really interested and impressed with my udacity capstone project the customer segmentation was relevant to the company and they liked my discussion of why i d made certain decisions when cleaning and analysing my datasets which i offered as the core reason i d been able to do so well in the kaggle contest i did my research and really dug into the company which is something i really enjoy doing anyway so that wasn t too difficult on monday i got a call from the recruiter saying the interview has gone well and i was their favoured candidate moving forward they were keen to move to a final stage test tuesday they give me a call and say they want to forgo the final test and make me a job offer today i signed and next monday i m starting as a data scientist i m absolutely ectastic and nervous with acute imposter syndrome but from what they want me to do i think i can do it and i m determined to demonstrate my value and secure my future in this field it s been an month journey and it s been tough if i hadn t had my partner to support me financially and emotionally i think i would have thrown in the towel i really don t envy any grads or people in my position but hopefully my experience is a useful insight happy to answer any questions courses i whole heartedly recommend full stack web development with react the hong kong university coursera javascript understanding the weird parts anthony alicea udemy data science a z kirill eremenko udemy python deep dive fred baptiste udemy build a saas app with flask nick janetakis udemy all the kaggle microcourses books i recommend deep learning with python effective python test driven development by kent beck sql practice problems bdd in action audiobooks the pragmatic programmer best of luck everyone']

Name: 0_code_ai_human_jobs
Representative_Docs: ['we in the business have heard similar for the last three decades and yet we re not a single step closer sorry to say but that teacher doesn t know zilch yes no code low code tools will improve in the future they will have their use case just as stock tools have their use cases yet specialized software will always need programmers last the ultimate question who will program maintain and improve the no code low code tools skynet an all knowing ai neither human programmers will you won t have to worry about no job opportunities throughout your entire career rather the opposite will happen there will be demand for more and more programmers but probably the specifications will shift mundane jobs will maybe be automated but specialized jobs will go in higher demand', 'sure but maybe development will become more accessible people struggle with learning react so maybe something that can make that process easier would be better for new developers here s the deal people are not rational and logical they want to build systems they can barely describe ai can t figure out what you want to build let s say you want to build a course registration system for students an ai won t know what a class is or what rules you want maybe you say i want students to only sign up for classes with no class conflict i want them to be dropped out if they fail a course unless they can get an exemption from a professor these are rules that may not even be that consistent that is you can have two rules that contradict one another and then you want a nice ui that isn t too confusing to people how is an ai going to figure that out in the past the solution was to describe these requirement in highly mathematical ways so maybe computers could figure it out but turns out it s hard for humans to describe things with this level of mathematical precision so they say something vague the ai doesn t under schools and classes and such so you d have to maybe program that in but that too is imprecise and then some people will want weird exceptions to rules people have been dreaming about this idea that is now called low code they once thought if you wrote programs using more english like cobol then people who weren t programmers could program then it became about writing rules for so called expert systems even low code which constrains what a web page can look like still involves some level of programming in the end even very bright people found they were still programming and then asked real programmers to write it because these systems would say doesn t make sense and they would have to fix it up that is they would have to debug it and these folks wanted to give some random gibberish and say you know what i mean and not have to improve the precision back when java was starting ibm had a product that eventually became eclipse it was an ide before that you compiled code the compiler had errors you went back to the editor to look at your code you tried to fix it then ran the compiler again and again but with an ide it would show your errors as you typed and you could fix it it would suggest method completions it would let you run the debugger hooked to the editor they used to be separate which made debugging a pain some may have thought well now everyone can program the ide is super intelligent and why do they need me while it did make programmers more productive programs also got bigger and more complicated so in a sense the problem got worse and developers had to do and know more just to develop programs that were considered too large could now be tackled leading to even bigger and more complicated programs in short people want programs to do things and they have no easy way to tell an ai in a precise enough way that an ai will crank out code this is why there was an idea to have ais know a lot of things like people do instead of only enough facts to do very specific jobs so a self driving car isn t going to tell you how to compose a song it s a specific kind of program for a specific kind of situation for more precise things e g chess or go where the rules are well known and you can compute things then you can have ais that try to train a machine to run an algorithm that a person didn t think about but games have qualities most software doesn t precise rules ways to tell who wins', 'u voldemortishere don t be i m not sure at what stage of career u commotum is or how much experience they have with software engineering in general if definitely seems like they are aboard the hype train it s very obvious that he is heavily invested in low code or just works at mendix based on his heavily marketing speech and personal attacks i ll address all the criticisms it s been years since someone quoted a forrester prediction and didn t do it as a joke or sarcastically since we are talking about data why not simply get data from linkedin or indeed searches for job availability and salaries for low code and traditional developer jobs research firm forrester estimated in that the total market for low code development platforms would grow to billion by did it no does it have jobs a b industry would have no low code isn t a very successful phenomenon is a very fair assessment low code is not something latest and greatest people have been hyped about it since s low code is also used as an buzz word the same way ai or blockchain is used different code generation and visual programming platforms rebrand themselves as low code the term itself doesn t mean anything specific it s a blur mx and low code in general is being adopted by the biggest corporations out there statement large corporations use a crazy number of technologies often redundant given enough time and you will see everything odd stack choices some off the shelf software a line long clojure script some guy wrote years ago if a company picks up mendix basic package or even the free plan for some inconsequential app means they are officially using mendix so these things do not mean much this does not mean they moved all their business applications and infrastructure to mendix now let s look at the career perspective i have no issue with mendix being a low code platform i have problem with it for being a proprietary and general purpose this has significant effects transfer of knowledge everything you learn with mendix is mendix related stays in mendix you cannot learn most of the software engineering by using mendix think about ruby on rails developers in late s if you knew ror and programming you can easily learn spring or django and still be productive in the field if you are a mendix dev if won t help you towards being a dev for a different low code platform everything is closed source and proprietary which means you as a developer are very prone to changes that mendix decides to make general purpose software is insanely difficult and has a lot of problems mendix is going down that rabbit hole low code platforms like node red appsmith or plotly dash are becoming more successful because they heavily use case specific and are fully open source this point has a lot to explain you ll learn these things eventually here is a good talk that can help you develop some perspective on it vendor lock in and expense you have to keep paying mendix to keep using and developing application per user per developer or pay a great deal for licensed nodes it s only a matter of time till it snowballs and management pulls the plug then they realize you can t port a mendix application you can t copy paste your business logic out of it a big downside of not being open source now let s look at downsides of being a dedicated low code developer u commotum guessed right i no longer work with siemens i work for a bank in tri state area and work on online low latency analytical and prediction engines when we hire senior engineers they are engineers they are not specifically a java developer or python developer experienced engineers are almost always polyglots and are not bound by tools they use because real engineering knowledge is transferable so you wouldn t want yourself to be bound to low code low bar dragging and dropping elements isn t exactly cerebrally intensive i m not going to pay my senior dev per hour to write a crud interface this kind of job will go to juniors or older associates who quite didn t make it at a cost of around per hour and eventually to an hour with that work being outsourced to bangalore work is work regardless how it pays but don t lock yourself on the low bar otherwise your career will be vertically challenged']

Name: 1_data_data science_science_data scientist
Representative_Docs: ['some of my friends from the communication field are telling me that if i didn t show any aptitude for stem back when i was younger it won t be manageable now and i should just give up this idea these people have no idea what the field entails and how to do it ignore them you reached out to data analysts and mathematicians and they said you could listen to those people because analytics is a field where you have to put in the time and effort to continually learn and grow there isn t an expectation that you are an expert as an entry level data analyst da if you are willing to put in the time and want to continually learn then yes go for this field if you are just in it because it s good money then i wouldn t do it because there are easier positions that pay as well i know i will probably never be brilliant in the field of data analysis don t say this data analytics is a field that takes years decades of learning to truly be a master of no one is going to expect you to be a master on year context of me i ve been trying to break into the data science field with mixed success myself i currently work as a data analyst but it is mainly using excel and record keeping i am competent in data analysis programming so i am currently looking for my first real analytical job w a goal of being a data scientist my advice is this first get a job as a data analyst this requires sql tableau no python r intense math data analyst mainly take data and create them into reports and dashboards python and r aren t used at this level because they are used for high level decision making once you get a da job using sql tableau start looking to build the necessary skills to become a data scientist ds ds jobs aren t jobs that you can jump into they usually expect a few years of da experience because there is an expectation that you will be able to jump in day and make good high level decisions for the business for learning ds you need to learn how to program well in python r know statistics linear algebra calculus well and machine learning once you have done', 'source of info landed my first data science role recently after management consultancy tech consultancy with a stint in stats modelling only have a bachelors but in maths just giving my cents sql is fundamental for all of the below so it s a good start i m going to consider data analyst and data science jobs separately any half respectable data science position will need you to be able to code in an object orientated language python and r are the most popular with python being favourite due to its open source support and can be more readily integrated into other platforms in addition heavy statistical knowledge is usually required for these positions which is why masters are usually required but bare minimum would be knowing how to implement ab testing especially in fmcg industry i would also say you should know about ml algorithms pros cons logic applications etc collaborative filtering and nlp basic concepts at least lastly industry expertise domain knowledge here can come in handy lots of the problems are around how can we better model understand situations so some subject matter expertise is always good if you re serious about data science i would advise more focus on the areas touched on above datacamp is good but coursera udacity udemy also have good free paid for courses data engineering is pretty much a pre requisite for this building apis data pipelines etl etc so would definitely get some intro to python and intro to machine learning under your belt considering data analyst jobs these can be pretty broad some companies just use sql with excel and vba if you know python r though you can do a lot more cool stuff in general my perception is that there s a lot more focus on data visualisation tableau power bi qlikview etc again if you want to generate more insights be an insight analyst some will require more statistical knowledge be careful though lots of jobs advertise using data scientist and data analyst interchangeably i m going to throw in data engineer or python developer in here as well the skill set is much the same as a data scientist minus the stats to get a good position in data science these fundamentals are going to help and may be a good stepping stone for you it ll focus more on coding and the infrastructure you can set up plus can be very lucrative without the need of more formal education', 'fwiw research based roles are what the job title data analyst do and have little to no overlap with engineering roles even ml engineering have you considered getting a data analyst certificate like the google one or similar and then applying for data analyst roles most data scientists were data analysts who got promoted btw edit post your resume on r dataanalyst and see if they ll give you some help when it comes to engineering related roles recruiters scan a resume in seconds usually seconds try scanning your resume at that speed and seeing what pops up what languages pop up for what projects i don t see anything that pops up you want projects relevant to what you re hiring for so using the same or a neighboring tech stack research positions don t mean anything for engineering so they should be minimized projects related to what you re applying for first recruiters don t understand what they re hiring for they only understand keywords eg programming language used in project research projects are data science related not engineering related if you want to do research related work for a living as a data scientist instead of as a grunt engineer go back to school get a phd though a masters may be enough if you have enough ds experience and make sure you know your statistics an alternative path is to apply to be a data analyst a data scientist is for all intents and purposes a senior data analyst that knows more programming if you get a job as a data analyst you can transfer over to data science that s how most data scientists get hired as a ds']

over k a year and offer a safe area thanks p s i need college recommendations that i can ed to as well i am currently doing virginia tech but everyone s telling me i m selling myself short and i should try washu but my gpa is ass so what do you guys think', 'hey everyone i m a nd year california cc student and i m currently in the process of applying to year colleges while i would have a bunch of great state schools in california to fall back on which i m planning on applying specifically to my local school csula cpp cpslo and sjsu and ucsb ucsd ucla and ucb i m more worried about applying for private schools or just applying in general given my circumstances at my community college i have a gpa with courses completed like calc iii differential equations linear algebra two semesters of calculus based physics solid mechanics engineering graphics materials science among other stem courses i currently have a outside research position as a visiting usc student in image machine learning and a prior internship working for the government with data analytics additionally i do photography on my spare time i m the offical photographer and social media marketing manager scaler for the football team i love creating cinematic films with my blackmagic pocket camera and post them online and i am passionate about creating a positive impact in low income communities by working with pro bono communities as i am myself a low income and first generation american and first generation college student on paper i sound like a good student that should have a chance at some top private schools the problem is the context i am diagnosed with autism adhd severe anxiety persistent depressive disorder and ptsd some since the age of so i ve always carried a neurodivergent label when the pandemic started i live in a studio apartment with two other family members and that was extremely difficult for me i couldn t concentrate at all with academics because everyone would make noise and when the pandemic first started everything was closed and i was honestly scared to go out anyways so i would have to use the restroom to do assignments or just have time to myself or i would suddenly break into meltdowns i couldn t see my friends at all because of covid when it happened and i felt extremely anxious i would get constant meltdowns panic attacks overstimulated then family issues arose and i was pinned in the middle of everything my close aunt and uncle suddenly got mad at me my entire family was dividing for something that was honestly trivial at best i was being told that i was worthless by some cousins it was painful for me it was too much for me to render all simultaneously i then tried ending myself one night around july but as you can probably tell that was unsuccessful i woke up in a hospital and i stayed there for a week i had to do an intensive therapy and group therapy for at least two weeks where i couldn t leave the hospital at all and then another two weeks where i was finally permitted to go to my house and be taken to sessions from that incident however my depression exacerbated i developed honestly weird fears looking at it now all my friends blocked me and kicked me out of their lives because they found out i was autistic from that incident and one of them told me we don t want to be friends with someone weird so i had no friends no one to talk to i was stuck in a tiny studio apartment with no privacy to myself my anxiety was at a all time high i felt horrible for letting discriminating and abelistic comments like your retarded ass will never be successful get to me so how does all of this relate to academics and the context as you can imagine trying to juggle my disabilities experiences depression and academics is quite a challenge let alone in a pandemic for the fall semester i was going to therapy sessions to help my depression and cope with my autism and adhd at least three times a week even though it was a lot and i was already going through a lot to begin with i had extremely and i mean extremely high expectations for myself and presumed i can manage both of my responsibilities halfway through the semester i had realized this was horrible idea and i should ve prioritized my mental health firstly i had three c s a f and only one a i had to get a withdrawal w for a class i knew i was for sure failing i ended up failing my first class ever with a np i got a a and a and then i got a c for another class but i took it as a pass grade instead because as many of you probably know many universities and colleges temporarily expanded the pass no pass grading system as a byproduct of covid spring semester came and my depression escalated i now had trouble trusting people at one point i stopped eating from random strangers such as restaurants because i thought the food and drinks were poisoned i still had no friends i had been virtually home for about seven months continously at this point i now had to do therapy for longer at least five times a week i still didn t know when to give up however i enrolled in another five courses and thought i could stick it out until the end i had an option of taking an exucsed leave of absence which looking back i should ve taken but i still decided to start what i eventually needed to finish by may i was finally getting progress done with my anxiety and depression i found that aforementioned love for photography and film and i started to for the first time in nearly a year see myself under a positive light i no longer saw disgust in every inch of myself because i was prioritizing my mental health so much this came at the cost of my grades for spring i thankfully passed all courses even the one i retook because i withdrew in fall which was calc unfortunately all my grades were horrible i had two c s one c a c and my highest grade was actually calc but just a b so to save my gpa i had to take each and every single one of my classes pass no pass so i have a semester on my transcript where i had units or courses that just have pass p grades additionally because i had nothing lined up for me besides filming documentaries for clients around downtown la in the summer i decided to take four summer school courses this past summer i took an introductory bio course that i got an a on an intro macro econ course i got an a on again the differential equations course i got an a on and a linear circuits course that was just way too hard for me and i got a b on and passed that one p instead so as you know now even though i have a gpa it would realistically be a or even less if it weren t for pass no pass grades i feel like i ruined my chances at top private colleges that i m actually intrigued by because i want to go for a blend of analytical business financial engineering or if no schools have that just industrial engineering as a last resort major carnegie mellon has a computational finance major that intrigues me a lot as i m interested in the field of practical interest in finance and numerical methods intersected with statistics and computatuonal methods which i would ve used to double major with a business or their unique artificial intelligence major columbia university similarly has a operations research financial engineering major that peaks my interest i would love to double major with their data science program and living in new york would be a dream come true for me even if only for a couple of years mit has a business analytics program that encompasses a blend of business big data and it s purpose in the business scope and computer science but all of that practically applied with business which is extremely exciting for me additionally they have a financial engineering research lab i would see myself performing research at constantly and their wise program sounds intriguing to me as a entrepreneur minded person usc where i m performing research at also has a computer science and business administration program while it sounds interesting to me it doesn t necessarily intersect and applies both of those fields together it s more of a double major of sorts but they have a financial engineering masters program and a bachelors masters program where i could get the fineng degree unfortunately it feels like i can only forget about those dreams of mine because it feels like i ruined my chances i know as a transfer these colleges have extremely low acceptance rates but with the staggering amount of pass grades i had to take it feels like i have no chance at this point this comes to the question of how would i justify all of this when applying for my transfer how would i write my application statements in a way where it doesn t sound like i couldn t handle the academic rigor of my courses i know i could ve easily gotten at least a b or even a s in courses i had to take pass grades for or withdraw under normal circumstances but of course what i had to go through were no normal circumstances where my grades i earned don t reflect my academic potential at all how could i write my essays in a way where it doesn t sound like i don t handle stress well which i don t but i don t want the admissions officers to know that part of me because that wouldn t look well at all how would i basically be able to most optimally justify me i obviously can t say things like i tried ending myself or i had to take intensive therapy to improve my mental health on my applications these past one in a half years have quite literally been the most intensive years of my life where i was pushed beyond my boundaries quite literally starred death in the eye but came through and became a better individual for myself i now live in a solo studio near my community college by myself so i won t get simulated by people or noises when studying i m working harder on academics and i m aiming for straight a s in my courses this semester i want to see if there s any advice overall for my application that i can work on or implement in addition to what i already have i would really love to see myself in a school like carngeie mellon or mit where i m taking advantage of the breadth of resources that interest me a bunch and i want to see if there are ways to tailor my application essays in a way that help me and not disclose sensitive information or if i simply don t have a chance at all', 'introduction i know a lot of you will be finalizing your college lists over the next couple months so i thought this might be helpful i also have posts discussing common misconceptions which i still see spread around here a lot and why not cmu this post purely discusses the positives of the university so i recommend you take a look at the other posts as well for context i am a sophomore in ece pursuing minors in hci and business currently i m very indecisive so that might change i visited cmu my junior year of high school and that visit is what made me ultimately decide not to apply however my parents made me apply really last minute and when making a final decision in april i came to realize that cmu is was a perfect fit for me so i urge you to take a closer look even if you don t love it immediately in no particular order here are some reasons to apply to cmu super interdisciplinary you can study anything at cmu it is very easy common and sometimes even required to combine your field of study with another interest if yours or to take courses in a department other than your primary department there are built in programs like bxa that combine fine arts majors with other majors across the university and singular interdisciplinary majors like computational finance psychology and biological sciences and computational biology additionally some majors like cs and business administration require you to complete a minor in a different department this is hands down my favorite thing about cmu and the main thing i was looking for in a college i m interested in a bunch of different things and cmu let s me explore all of them and combine them all together for example hci let s me combine cs psych business design and decision sciences to form one cohesive minor stellar academics cmu has top ranked and otherwise highly regarded programs in almost every discipline notably acting design ece cs business and many many more not only can you explore different fields like the previous point but you re at one of the best universities in the world for all those different fields this also makes it a great place to be if you re undecided the quality of the education is incredible and the the curriculum s rigor is rivaled by few unique majors the best example of this is the ai major cmu is one of the only schools if not the only school in the country that offers a major in ai this lets you focus on a really specific field and get a really deep understanding of it there s probably a few more examples of unique majors but i m too lazy to look them up the point is you can learn something with a depth that broader majors like just cs at other universities don t necessarily have amazing job outcomes just look at the salary data i think this speaks for itself a lot of people talk a lot about different reasons to go to different universities but in the end the point of college for most people is to get a higher paying job in a field that they enjoy specifically for software jobs which i know the most about because that happens to be what i am pursuing the cmu name alone will open a lot of doors just head over to r csmajors and take a look cmu is grouped with stanford and mit which together form the top cs schools additionally cmu sends the highest percent of their graduates to faang of any other school again jobs money aren t everything but they certainly are a very important factor great social life this is one of the biggest misconceptions i see on this subreddit my social life is so much better at cmu then it really has ever has been before there are a decent amount parties and people do fun things just like any students at other university do you really think year old students are gonna sit in their rooms and study all weekend every weekend people seem to think that because cmu is hard it s impossible to do anything else no one does anything else but that s certainly not true ece is considered one of the harder majors at cmu and last year i managed to maintain a very high gpa though gpa kinda doesn t matter unless you want to go to grad school and i didn t do work on a single weekend like not one singular bit on any weekend while this might change for me in the future by planning carefully you will definitely be able to manage the course load well and have a decent amount of free time flexible programs like i said earlier cmu allows for a lot of exploration and study outside of your primary major every college has a different set of geneds too but i ll provide engineering as an example for engineering i only need to take gened courses and there is a large list of courses to choose from for many of those on top of this there is free electives built into the curriculum where you are allowed to take any courses you want from across the university this allows for a very flexible program of study and also allows room for minors additional majors also you can take classes at any of the pche colleges in pittsburgh so you have a massive number of courses to choose from nice campus a pretty campus was very important to me when choosing a college i think cmu is really beautiful see here and i can t explain the amount of serotonin i get from walking across campus on a nice day when choosing a college i was very conflicted between wanting a college in a city and wanting a traditional college campus cmu is both it s a traditional college campus located in the city which i love additionally it s a walkable distance from other universities pitt right next door chatham on the other side and carlow on the other side of pitt medium size i don t have too much to say about this but it s nice to have a large variety of people but relatively small majors to get to know people well it s also pretty easy to get the classes you want and guaranteed that you get the classes you need because departments reserve seats for you research study abroad opportunities lots of research in pretty much every discipline there is a lot of funding for undergrads to complete whatever research they want and there are also opportunities to work with professors on their research for pay or for course credit study abroad opportunities are cool and sometimes really easy because cmu has campuses in other countries so you can directly transfer grades and credits there s also unique programs like tcingc which is a technology internship abroad paid for by cmu separate departments allow for great depth while some schools will have one mammoth department with faculty specializing in different areas cmu has entire different departments the largest example of this is the school of computer science while they have a cs department they also have a department for software engineering isr one for ml one for ai one for hci hcii one for language technologies lti one for robotics robotics institute etc they have a large amount of faculty that specialize in each area and each department offers their own courses minors and research opportunities so you can get a lot of depth along with a lot of breadth sometimes good financial aid and merit scholarships this is one of the wackier points some people get amazing financial aid and some people get terrible financial aid it is really hit or miss personally cmu really exceeded my expectations for financial aid and it was one of my cheapest options however an interesting point is that cmu is one of the only top schools offering merit scholarships also take this with a grain of salt because this is just what happened to me and what i was told cmu says that they don t offer scholarships on their website i didn t know about this until two weeks ago freshman year i received a scholarship on top of my other grants that i assumed was just financial aid but this year my financial aid got messed up and i was supposed to get no financial aid at all but i still received the k i met with the financial aid office and they told me that it was a scholarship guaranteed for all years regardless of my financial need they also promptly fixed my financial aid issue i think this is weird because cmu explicitly says on their website that they don t offer scholarships aside outside of demonstrated financial need super collaborative cmu is a very collaborative school it s not cutthroat at all and courses are only ever curved to benefit the students if they are curved at all don t make the mistake of confusing a difficult university with a cutthroat one i see this happen with berkeley a lot which i ve heard from students is also not cutthroat doesn t try to be exclusive this is a weirder point and i wasn t sure how to word it but i think it s an important one i know some universities don t increase their size just so they can remain competitive have low acceptance rates but cmu doesn t do that recently the is major has quadrupled in size the acceptance rate used to hover around and now it is i really appreciate how cmu tries to accommodate as many students as possible into their field of interest i ve heard that the cs department will also be expanding soon working for social good this is also one of the more unique points i ve found but a lot of cmu s work focuses on social good impactful work courses like ai methods for social good engineering and social justice etc are offered and integrated into regular coursework as well see here there is a lot of opportunities here to make your work impactful regardless of your major extremely innovative lastly cmu is incredibly innovative some really cool things have come out of cmu and some really cool things are currently being made here some of my favorites and some of the most popular are duolingo you all know what this is actually a lot of cmu students are hired to work there after graduation astrobotic essentially an interplanetary mail service started as a club at cmu and now receives multimillion dollar contracts from nasa and baymax the robot from big hero was inspired by a robotics project here at cmu and is being worked on here it s just really cool being at a place creating so many new things and being able to take part myself conclusion there s a lot more things i could talk about like the fun traditions the passionate students etc but i feel like i ve covered the main points feel free to ask any questions at all i look forward to answering them you can also dm pm me for anything']

Name: 4_deep learning_learning_deep_book
Representative_Docs: ['the machine learning course of andrew ng on coursera is amazing good mix between tgeory and practical use you have to pay if you want the certificate but you can follow the lessons for free the deep learning bundle of courses related to it is great as well edit for the frameworks keras and tensorflow are great to look into for neural networks in python scikit learn offers great functions for things like clustering random forest pytorch seems great as welk but my personal experience with it is virtually non existent', 'depends on so many factors are you just looking to write deep learning algorithms or are you actually interested in research theory what sort of math are you comfortable with can you read amp write proofs you re probably going to see the book deep learning by goodfellow et al mentioned here or elsewhere keep in mind it s pretty math heavy and probably won t really help you write code you ll need to know calculus and at least linear algebra that being said it s a great book if you re looking for theory and math honestly if you re not comfortable with multivariable calculus and linear algebra i wouldn t suggest you start learning deep learning at all until those are familiar to you it would also behoove you to have a solid foundation in mathematical statistics and probability assuming you have the necessary background in mathematics i would recommend the following artificial intelligence a modern approach by stuart russell and peter norvig will provide a broad overview of the theory and practice of artificial intelligence generally the elements of statistical learning would be a good place to go from there the aforementioned deep learning book by goodfellow et al after those look for something practical someone here mentioned a book they wrote always a good idea to support the reddit community there s also hands on machine learning deep learning with python grokking deep learning', 'i m a hobbysit civil engr not a software engineer also terrible at math but my cents i ve taken the stanford machine learning and deeplearning ai deep learning specialization courses on coursera and found them to be pretty decent andrew ng explains things in pretty simple terms so it s easy to follow along or at least get the main concepts the stanford course use octive matlab and feels a bit dated now but is still a good intro imo and focuses on theory side deeplearning ai you get a bit more familiar with tensor flow keras sequential functional apis and work in jupyter notebooks if you take that specialization you begin to appreciate ml s many applications and its interesting to get a bit of the background of how the models were developed and their different architectures still leaves something to be desired though you re not going to be an expert after taking those courses they simply give you a better idea what s out there and the challenges involved i really liked the modules content on transfer learning once you realize how much work goes into curating your data and training a big model the idea of borrowing parts of a retrained model and adapting it to your application is neat overall i think you will get more out of learning by applying it to an actual project you re interested in i think with the basic concepts most libraries have sufficient docs for you to jump right in i also took the umich applied ml in python course part of their data science specialization that one is taught using the scikit learn library course was ok but the applied aspect definitely got me comfortable enough to use it in my actual job for building some classifiers regressors these were all classes that came at the recommendation of reddit when i was snooping around a year or so ago for books i m still working through these but i ve had colleagues in my company cite from aurelien gerons hands on machine learning with scikit keras and tensorflow there is also deep learning with python or r by francois chollet who is a contributor developer for tensorflow there is some good convo reading on stanford s cs page as well']

Name: 5_interview_questions_interviews_leetcode
Representative_Docs: ['dear colleagues tldr what is different about data science consulting interview process compared to a non consulting interview and how to prepare for one any resources are much appreciated i am turning to you especially those who work or have worked in ml consulting for some interview prep advice so far my professional experience constitutes of working in marketing where i used data to drive business decisions for yrs then data analytics for yr and machine learning yrs i believe my experience sets me up well for a job in consulting which i want to pursue because i think i can be good with clients and i am still young and would love to take this opportunity to keep exploring my career paths so i can guess i will have to do some aptitude test and i will have a technical interview where they will try to gauge my understanding of various models ask me about bias variance trade off and similar questions for this i am reading the introduction to statistical learning book taking notes and preparing model answers do you have any advice on how i can increase my chance of success in this part of the interview what i don t know is how the consulting interview may be different to a standard ml interview where one would do ml for the company internally i am guessing there may be a case study based interview would anyone be able to tell me more about this and how i can prepare for it also would they test my programming knowledge is it worth me practicing programming problems online or are they only used for software engineering positions what else may be different about the ml consulting interview compared to a standard one', 'recruiters spend second reading resumes and skimming cover letters takes longer than resumes they aren t digesting the cover letter information much if they even look before filtering by resume quality especially since cover letters typically have a lot of fluff and are usually similar to each other in core content even if one is different from the rest that difference is easy to miss while rapidly looking through them let me walk through a common scenario i ve seen as a hiring manager and heard from recruiter friends you can choose different numbers but every probable assumption from situations i ve seen from being inside the hiring process still favors skipping cover letters imagine receiving applications with cover letter for a position the hiring manager wants asap say a cover letter takes minutes to read and think critically about its content you can spend six hours reading every cover letter or you can filter out resumes that don t have a skillset or professional background the hiring manager will consider before glancing at them after the filter you have applications with cover letters on the shortlist and time for introduction calls to determine if the person is a good fit you still want to rank them by their resume quality if you want to maximize the chance they ll pass a technical onsite to avoid wasting employee time so they keep using your service and get your commission sooner if you re willing to favor applications with a cover letter that have resumes implying a lower chance at passing the onsite you may choose of the applications with cover letters and of the ones without them this results in interviews with cover letter applications and without in this scenario writing a cover letter doubled response rates in the time each of those applicants wrote their cover letter the others without them must have been able to do at least applications that s a serious underestimation since is quite possible at that time on the low end people skipping cover letters get at least twice as many introduction interviews with this situation repeating at other companies and four times as many on the high end i haven t written a cover letter since my first job search during that having a cover letter seemed to increase my response rate by perhaps i can do applications while customizing my resume for each job since i keep a copy of each customized resume then use the best matching one i already made as a base when customizing to save time my first job took applications i used a cover letter on of them the companies that responded to my applications with cover letters had worse cultures and pay than the ones that didn t since then i ve had three other jobs and will accept another next week without writing any cover letters i ve done applications in that time and got interviews with on sites as a tangent my strategy is getting moderately skilled at least top at uncommon combinations in my free time and looking for places that need it being in the top of people who do uncommon combination is easier than getting in than being in the top of people who specialize in any single one of them from lower competition volume many people can do firmware modify an android os can do full stack application development or have tinkered with d graphics programming i got my second job by being mildly above average at all four which is extremely rare compared to people that can only do one so being in the top of each put you in the top of people who do all three competently jobs that need it are uncommon but you only need that want it since your response rate will be close to my second job came from doing reinforcement learning inside projects and being knowledgeable in psychology and learning science at an armchair hobbyist level my third was knowing low level c optimization from android os work machine learning experience strong python skills from ml in the previous job and a working knowledge of web security etc for the next two in any case good luck to everyone searching tl dr i recommend skipping cover letters getting good at somewhat unrelated skills and finding jobs that need that uncommon combination edit i have a note on what you specifically said as a hiring manager how much you want the job only matters once i m convinced you can do the job and fit into the team culture well i decide how much they want the job i e whether they ll be engaged and likely to stay more than a year or two during my interviews with them and feedback from my team faking genuine interest in a cover letter is easier than doing it face to face and i m not interested in applicants who desperately want the job if they don t look like they ll do it well', 'edit i m on discord to answer your questions it s pretty late in the uk pm but i ll stay up as long as you guys want me to organisation is key i ve interviewed at google and deepmind uber facebook amazon for roles that lie under the data scientist umbrella and this is the typical interview construction theme i ve observed software engineering applied statistics machine learning data wrangling manipulation and visualisation now nobody is expecting some super graduate level competency in all of these topics but you need to know enough to convince your interviewer that you re capable of delivering if they offered you the job how much you need to know depends on the job spec but in this increasingly competitive market no knowledge is lost i recommend using notion to organise your job prep it s extremely versatile and enables you to utilise the spaced repetition and active recall principles to nail down learning and deploying key topics that come up time and time again in a data scientist interview ali abdaal has a great tutorial on note taking with notion to maximise your learning potential during the interview process i used to run through my notion notes over and over but in particular right before my interview this ensured that key topics and definitions were loaded into my working memory and i didn t waste precious time ummmmmm ing when hit with some question software engineering not all data scientist roles will grill you on the time complexity of an algorithm but all of these roles will expect you to write code data science isn t one job but a collection of jobs that attracts talent from a variety of industries including the software engineering world as such you re competing with guys that know the ins and outs of writing efficient code and i would recommend spending at least hours a day in the lead up to your interview practicing the following concepts arrays hash tables linked lists two pointer based algorithms string algorithms interviewers love these binary search divide and conquer algorithms sorting algorithms dynamic programming recursion do not learn the algorithms off by heart this approach is useless because the interviewer can question you on any variation of the algorithm and you will be lost instead learn the strategy behind how each algorithm works learn what computational and spatial complexity are and learn why they are so fundamental to building efficient code leetcode was my best friend during interview preparation and is well worth the per month in my opinion your interviewers only have so many algorithm questions to sample from and this website covers a host of algorithm concepts including companies that are likely or are known to have asked these questions in the past there s also a great community who discuss each problem in detail and helped me during the myriad of stuck moments i encountered leetcode has a lite version with a smaller question bank if the price tag is too steep as do hackerrank and geeksforgeeks which are other great resources what you should do is attempt each question even if it s a brute force approach that takes ages to run then look at the model solution and try to figure out what the optimal strategy is then read up what the optimal strategy is and try to understand why this is the optimal strategy ask yourself questions like why is quicksort o n average time complexity why do two pointers and one for loop make more sense than three for loops applied statistics data science has an implicit dependence on applied statistics and how implicit that will be depends on the role you ve applied for where do we use applied statistics it pops up just about anywhere where we need to organise interpret and derive insights from data i studied the following topics intensely during my interviews and you bet your bottom dollar that i was grilled about each topic descriptive statistics what distribution does my data follow what are the modes of the distribution the expectation the variance probability theory given my data follows a binomial distribution what is the probability of observing paying customers in click through events hypothesis testing forming the basis of any question on a b testing t tests anova chi squared tests etc regression is the relationship between my variables linear what are potential sources of bias what are the assumptions behind the ordinary least squares solution bayesian inference what are some advantages disadvantages vs frequentist methods if you think this is a lot of material you are not alone i was massively overwhelmed with the volume of knowledge expected in these kinds of interviews and the plethora of information on the internet that could help me two invaluable resources come to mind when i was revising for interviews introduction to probability and statistics an open course on everything listed above including questions and an exam to help you test your knowledge machine learning a bayesian and optimization perspective by sergios theodoridis this is more a machine learning text than a specific primer on applied statistics but the linear algebra approaches outlined here really help drive home the key statistical concepts on regression the way you re going to remember this stuff isn t through memorisation you need to solve as many problems as you can get your hands on glassdoor is a great repo for the sorts of applied stats questions typically asked in interviews the most challenging interview i had by far was with g research but i really enjoyed studying for the exam and their sample exam papers were fantastic resources when it came to testing how far i was getting in my applied statistics revision machine learning now we come to the beast the buzzword of our millennial era and a topic so broad that it can be easy to get so lost in revision that you want to give up the applied statistics part of this study guide will give you a very very strong foundation to get started with machine learning which is basically just applied applied statistics written in fancy linear algebra but there are certain key concepts that came up over and over again during my interviews here is a by no means exhaustive set of concepts organised by topic metrics classification confusion matrices accuracy precision recall sensitivity f score tpr tnr fpr fnr type i and type ii errors auc roc curves metrics regression total sum of squares explained sum of squares residual sum of squares coefficient of determination and its adjusted form aic and bic advantages and disadvantages of rmse mse mae mape bias variance tradeoff over under fitting k nearest neighbours algorithm and the choice of k in bias variance trade off random forests the asymptotic property curse of dimensionality model selection k fold cross validation l and l regularisation bayesian optimization sampling dealing with class imbalance when training classification models smote for generating pseudo observations for an underrepresented class class imbalance in the independent variables sampling methods sources of sampling bias measuring sampling error hypothesis testing this really comes under under applied statistics but i cannot stress enough the importance of learning about statistical power it s enormously important in a b testing regression models ordinary linear regression its assumptions estimator derivation and limitations are covered in significant detail in the sources cited in the applied statistics section other regression models you should be familiar with are deep neural networks for regression random forest regression xgboost regression time series regression arima sarima bayesian linear regression gaussian process regression clustering algorithms k means hierarchical clustering dirichlet process mixture models classification models logistic regression most important one revise well multiple regression xgboost classification support vector machines it s a lot but much of the content will be trivial if your applied statistics foundation is strong enough i would recommend knowing the ins and outs of at least three different classification regression clustering methods because the interviewer could always and has previously asked what other methods could we have used what are some advantages disadvantages this is a small subset of the machine learning knowledge in the world but if you know these important examples the interviews will flow a lot more smoothly data manipulation and visualisation what are some of the steps for data wrangling and data cleaning before applying machine learning algorithms we are given a new dataset the first thing you ll need to prove is that you can perform an exploratory data analysis eda before you learn anything realise that there is one path to success in data wrangling pandas the pandas ide when used correctly is the most powerful tool in a data scientists toolbox the best way to learn how to use pandas for data manipulation is to download many many datasets and learn how to do the following set of tasks as confidently as you making your morning cup of coffee one of my interviews involved downloading a dataset cleaning it visualising it performing feature selection building and evaluating a model all in one hour it was a crazy hard task and i felt overwhelmed at times but i made sure i had practiced building model pipelines for weeks before actually attempting the interview so i knew i could find my way if i got lost advice the only way to get good at all this is to practice and the kaggle community has an incredible wealth of knowledge on mastering edas and model pipeline building i would check out some of the top ranking notebooks on some of the projects out there download some example datasets and build your own notebooks get familiar with the pandas syntax data organisation there are three sure things in life death taxes and getting asked to merge datasets and perform groupby and apply tasks on said merged datasets pandas is incredibly versatile at this so please practice practice practice data profiling this involves getting a feel for the meta characteristics of the dataset such as the shape and description of numerical categorical and date time features in the data you should always be seeking to address a set of questions like how many observations do i have what does the distribution of each feature look like what do the features mean this kind of profiling early on can help you reject non relevant features from the outset such as categorical features with thousands of levels names unique identifiers and mean less work for you and your machine later on work smart not hard or something woke like that data visualisation here you are asking yourself what does the distribution of my features even look like a word of advice if you didn t learn about boxplots in the applied statistics part of the study guide then here is where i stress you learn about them because you need to learn how to identify outliers visually and we can discuss how to deal with them later on histograms and kernel density estimation plots are extremely useful tools when looking at properties of the distributions of each feature we can then ask what does the relationship between my features look like in which case python has a package called seaborn containing very nifty tools like pairplot and a visually satisfying heatmap for correlation plots handling null values syntax errors and duplicate rows columns missing values are a sure thing in any dataset and arise due to a multitude of different factors each contributing to bias in their own unique way there is a whole field of study on how best to deal with missing values and i once had an interview where i was expected to know individual methods for missing value imputation in much detail check out this primer on ways of handling null values syntax errors typically arise when our dataset contains information that has been manually input such as through a form this could lead us to erroneously conclude that a categorical feature has many more levels than are actually present because hot hot hot n are all considered unique levels check out this primer on handling dirty text data finally duplicate columns are of no use to anyone and having duplicate rows could lead to overrepresentation bias so it s worth dealing with them early on standardisation or normalisation depending on the dataset you re working with and the machine learning method you decide to use it may be useful to standardize or normalize your data so that different scales of different variables don t negatively impact the performance of your model there s a lot here to go through but honestly it wasn t as much the memorise everything mentality that helped me insofar as it was the confidence building that learning as much as i could instilled in me i must have failed so many interviews before the formula clicked and i realised that all of these things aren t esoteric concepts that only the elite can master they re just tools that you use to build incredible models and derive insights from data best of luck on your job quest guys if you need any help at all please let me know and i will answer emails questions when i can']

Representative_Docs: ['bootcamp grads are also on here all of the time so are self taught developers there are most definitely more bootcamp grads without jobs than cs grads no doubt about it there s a reason for what i said in my first post about being a successful bootcamp graduate there s a lot of people that just aren t cut out for it that go to bootcamps a bigger payout is a myth i personally know bootcamp grads at google making way more than a buddy with a cs degree it s all about landing the job at the end of the day and these jobs are the same for bootcamp grads self taught devs and cs graduates in fact a bootcamp grad with years of experience can demand and will make a lot more money than a cs graduate with no experience and those are just the facts experience trumps everything in this field now if you re talking about other fields like maybe game development ml ai embedded etc that s a completely different topic', 'would i be dumb to study one of these subjects if i want to do
