Name: 60_engineers_people_tech_average
Representative_Docs: ['they burn out of big tech and go to more stable companies fang is a young man s game although i did know a couple incredibly talented graybeards during my stint at one there is a noticeable gap between say mid s and mid s after the kids are in college then sometimes family men go chase their ambitions in the last decade of their career plus college is fucking expensive there s a lot of ageism and a lot of people move onto new careers too but a lot of it is just realizing chasing tc isn t worth missing out on watching your kids grow up you ll also notice if you re in a big tech hub there aren t many middle class families left in your neighborhood the children are either coming from working class or poor families or very rich families and make no mistake ics are middle class not very rich you also have to understand there simply weren t that many swes in of the ones that were many stuck with the more legacy tech there s still demand for mainframe engineers c c and java it might not be as high as the demand for what s new and sexy but the talent pool is also smaller why would you start from scratch on a brand new stack when there s still high demand for what you re an expert in gt i m sure there isn t enough group manager and higher management roles to accommodate the amount of young people here once they grow older the current rate of growth is probably untenable remote work and ai will probably shrink the demand for swes in a decade don t blow your money on teslas and dos comas properties in california but i don t have a crystal ball so who knows make hay while the sun shines and also consider many people have no desire to go into management they wind up starting their own businesses remaining an ic going into research pivoting to a tangential field like infosec joining an early startup or becoming goat farmers personally management is the last thing i want to do i don t like hierarchy i m here to get my fu money and make my next move maybe it ll be a workers co op who knows', 'first you re referring to ilya sutskever m salary he was not recently hired he s a cofounder director and chief scientist of openai he gets articles written about him in magazines a big part of his salary is his celebrity and personal relationships with elon musk andrew ng and other powerful people in the tech industry such a hire is more like hiring dwayne johnson he gets a big salary not because he s the best actor in the world but because of his brand there really aren t a whole lot of good clear objective metrics of skill and productivity businesses have many different hiring amp performance evaluation practices and none of them have strong evidential support demonstrating predictive power for productivity someone with a real definitive provable answer for what separates an average engineer from an amazing one could probably start a billion dollar recruiting training and or consulting firm some people think a x engineer is one who memorizes every obscure algorithm pattern framework api and niche behavior relevant to the technology you re working with while being a full time puzzle solver perhaps they re a math savant or industry level expert in a particular field it s the master of one approach others take the jack of all trades approach having a rare combination of technical personal organizational analytical etc skills that makes them capable of insights others aren t personally i think the best engineers are the ones who make everyone around them a little better helping the team behave as a cohesive whole leveraging the power of human cooperation having a lot of lenses you can apply to problem solving is very helpful a broad array of perspectives allows you to find the path of least resistance to a solution engineers that understand systems thinking have an advantage i can take the same paper and make a paper airplane box or one of those fortune tellers same material completely different behavior i can take the same wood and make a shed or a raft same material completely different behavior it s the same for programming for teams of of people for business practices a good engineer understands not only the parts technical amp design proficiency but the whole systems processes people business requirements not only the how but the why in a way that s almost zen or taoistic', 'man there is so much wrong in these posts here lmao let me clear some of this stuff up there really isn t a concept of genius programmers the people that are scientifically brilliant that do computing are generally mathematicians or ee first programmers second and are working in areas such as machine learning in both mathematics to optimize speed up learning and software hardware in order to come up with specific chip design to facilitate faster machine learning like google tpus the thing that separates very good programmers from very bad is the resolution of their knowledge an analogy is the difference between knowing directions to places in terms of turns versus having a complete mental image of the map with every single street and using that to figure out the directions for any given problem even ones you haven t seen before what you call genius programmers is someone who can write verilog code to make cpu write assembly code for that cpu to make a notional bootloader write a compiler for that cpu and then write an entire os with interface to a graphics and perhiperals like a gpu then write a networking stack then compile stuff like node js to write a web app e t c and so on thing is anyone who is technically competent in one area can achieve this level because all it requires is just experience in doing all of those incremental steps and then eventually you realize how it all fits together faang developers aren t good by any means they are generally better than the average but that is because the average is quite low most modern software work involves copypasting or reusing already written code with a few changes for which you don t need much resolution of the knowledge domain faang on the average generally slightly above this but there are teams at faang that do very basic shit and teams that do very advanced shit the general hiring process tends to select for people who are able to demonstrate that they somewhat understand the outline of the map instead of just being able to recite directions from memory nobody smart goes to work for the us government or contractors of the salaries are significantly lower the bureaucracy is way higher wanna get promoted all about who Name: 61_python_resources_learning resources_testing
Representative_Docs: ['i have a comprehensive collection of python learning resources here includes books courses exercises projects debugging testing cheatsheets algorithms domain specific resources like machine learning web development data science etc', 'i have a blog post i know python basics what next that has resource links for exercises projects debugging testing intermediate advanced python algorithms design patterns cheatsheets etc i m also building a collection of searchable python learning resources this includes domain specific resources like machine learning web development data science etc', 'what to do next after learning python basics is an often asked question searching for what next on r learnpython gives you too many results here s some wonderful articles on this topic i know how to program but i don t know what to program things you might encounter in your programming journey techniques for efficiently learning programming languages exercises and projects i do not have a simple answer to this question either if you feel comfortable with programming basics and python syntax then exercises are a good way to test your knowledge the resource you used to learn python will typically have some sort of exercises so those would be ideal as a first choice i d also suggest using the below resources to improve your skills if you get stuck reread the material related to those topics search online ask for clarifications etc in short make an effort to solve it it is okay to skip some troublesome problems and come back to it later if you have the time but you should be able to solve most of the beginner problems maintaining notes and cheatsheets will help too especially for common mistakes exercism practicepython edabit these are all beginner friendly and difficulty levels are marked page python intro exercises exercises from my introductory guide codewars adventofcode projecteuler more challenging checkio codingame codecombat gaming based challenges r dailyprogrammer not active currently but there s plenty of past challenges with discussions once you are comfortable with basics and syntax the next step is projects i use a line program that solves a common problem for me adding body text align justify to epub files that are not justify aligned i didn t know that this line would help beforehand found a solution online and then automated the process of unzipping epub adding the line and then packing it again that will likely need you to lookup documentation and go through some stackoverflow q amp a as well and once you have written the solution and use it regularly you ll likely encounter corner cases and features to be added i feel this is a great way to learn and understand programming practice python projects my book on beginner to intermediate level projects projects with solutions algorithms data structures networking security databases etc project based learning web applications bots data science machine learning etc pytudes by peter norvig python programs usually short of considerable difficulty books the big book of small python projects tiny python projects impractical python projects and real world python r learnpython what do you automate with python at home debugging knowing how to debug your programs is crucial and should be ideally taught right from the beginning instead of a chapter at the end of the book think python is an awesome example for such a resource material sites like pythontutor allow you to visually debug a program you can execute a program step by step and see the current value of variables similar feature is typically provided by ides like pycharm and thonny under the hood these visualizations are using the pdb module see also python debugging with pdb debugging is often a frustrating experience taking a break helps and sometimes i find the solution or spot a problem in my dreams try to reduce the code as much as possible so that you are left with minimal code necessary to reproduce the issue talking about the problem to a friend colleague inanimate objects etc can help too known as rubber duck debugging i have often found the issue while formulating a question to be asked on forums like stackoverflow reddit because writing down your problem is another way to bring clarity than just having a vague idea in your mind here s some more articles on this challenging topic what does debugging a program look like how to debug small programs debugging guide problem solving skills here s an interesting snippet paraphrased from a collection of interesting bug stories gt a jpeg parser choked whenever the ceo came into the room because he always had a shirt with a square pattern on it which triggered some special case of contrast and block boundary algorithms see also this curated list of absurd software bug stories testing another crucial aspect in the programming journey is knowing how to write tests in bigger projects usually there are separate engineers often in much larger number than code developers to test the code even in those cases writing a few sanity test cases yourself can help you develop faster knowing that the changes aren t breaking basic functionality there s no single consensus on test methodologies there is unit testing integration testing test driven development and so on often a combination of these is used these days machine learning is also being considered to reduce the testing time see testing firefox more efficiently with machine learning for example when i start a project i usually try to write the programs incrementally say i need to iterate over files from a directory i will make sure that portion is working usually with print statements then add another feature say file reading and test that and so on this reduces the burden of testing a large program at once at the end and depending upon the nature of the program i ll add a few sanity tests at the end for example for my command help project i copy pasted a few test runs of the program with different options and arguments into a separate file and wrote a program to perform these tests programmatically whenever the source code is modified for non trivial projects you ll usually end up needing frameworks like built in module unittest or third party modules like pytest here s some learning resources getting started with testing in python python testing style guide calmcode pytest tdd in python with pytest obeythetestinggoat tdd for the web with python selenium django javascript and pals modern test driven development in python tdd guide has a real world application example intermediate to advanced python resources official python docs python docs are a treasure trove of information calmcode videos on testing code style args kwargs data science etc practical python programming covers foundational aspects of python programming with an emphasis on script writing data manipulation and program organization beyond the basic stuff with python best practices tools and techniques oop practice projects fluent python takes you through python s core language features and libraries and shows you how to make your code shorter faster and more readable at the same time serious python deployment scalability testing and more practices of the python pro learn to design professional level clean easily maintainable software at scale includes examples for software development best practices algorithms and design patterns problem solving with algorithms and data structures classic computer science problems in python deepens your knowledge of problem solving techniques from the realm of computer science by challenging you with time tested scenarios exercises and algorithms github collection of design patterns and idioms architecture patterns with python enabling tdd ddd and event driven microservices handy cheatsheets python crash course cheatsheet comprehensive python cheatsheet scientific python cheatsheet common beginner errors use the pdf link more python resources inspired by this post i made a python learning resources repository which is categorized beginner intermediate advanced domains like web ml data science etc and includes a handy search feature i hope these resources will help you take that crucial next step and continue your python journey happy learning']

Name: 62_new_years_going_software
Representative_Docs: ['yes and no most people you see here on code camps and a whole bunch of places including university seats the vast majority of them will quit the demand for really good really competent devs will never go away well not until we manage to make ai that is smart enough to program itself buuuuuut then we ll have other problems to worry about and not jobs not money cuz that will be in like years if we re lucky the demand for low end devs fluctuates a lot it seems to be economy crash proof doesn t really matters what happens when and where the solution will still require software to some degree will still require devs it s not going away not fading not a buble it s omnipresent and it is the future that being said low end devs that do menial work specialize in one area and which are a quantity over quality sort of thing those jobs do fluctuate with the economy economy great will hire loads of juniors and people with no xp economy shit time to trim the fat tbh qa and hr will be trimmed before most devs but when it comes to trimming devs very rarely will the firm let go the years of xp architect they spent years recruiting that guy can keep a project afloat alone while juniors couldn t possibly fill his shoes so as a conclusion if your real fear is yourself and not necessarily programming as a whole then you should consider your options do you have the dedication time and predisposition to go the long haul or not if not you ll get a job probably better than most but you ll be in the part that will get cut loose in a crisis i highly recommend going the long route if you have the time and mental capacity not as in intelligence but are you tired after work are you mentally tired then learning is really difficult if you think you can crack it do it properly study your ass off don t just learn a language learn esoteric concepts learn how computers think learn algorithms data structures design patterns get a deep understanding of the underlying structures if you can do that you ll probably be in the first category recession proof jobs for life not as in this job for life but if something happens i ll have a new job within a month during the worst economic recession in the past years', 'lol if you re not big on continuing education you may want to reconsider computer science as a field of study as an software engineer you re going to constantly have to unskill to understand new shifts in technology e g the rise of cloud architectures new languages new devices and even programming paradigms as someone thay works in ml ai the things i ve seen make me think this curve is only going to get steeper the continuing education you ll need to stay competitive and relevant as a swe may come from a graduate degree but more likely it ll come from self learning and on the job work as cs depts typically do a horrible job keeping up with what the industry actually needs either way if you re going to work in this industry you should be aware that you ll constantly need to be learning something new to keep up at least for most roles to answer your question directly yeah it looks that way but then again it should this is a natural consequence of the progression of science economics and society as a whole as technology advances with time it takes more and more education to do jobs effectively think about how much there is to know about medicine nowadays as compared to or even years ago while this may lead to inflation of degrees needed to do a job i m quite glad that nurses and doctors have to learn more more knowledge means better care and better outcomes there s no magic rule that says we get to be done with learning after earning a year degree education is about getting you to a place where you can do the job as the jobs change the education requirements change if anyone wants to plant their flag and be done with learning after years they can nothing is stopping them but they shouldn t whine when your skill set becomes irrelevant and your wages drop it becomes harder to land a job etc they can t have it both ways jobs that aren t changing are the ones that are going going get automated away first', 'will the programming landscape leave the majority of your education behind absolutely it will in fact probably happen in the span it takes you to start your matriculation to the day you graduate will that cause a contraction in the software development market anyone who tells you with any certainty what will happen one way or another is full of bupkis however if you go with the way things are going now it s highly highly unlikely and there is nothing even on the distant horizon that can realistically be expected to crush the software engineering industry this is a common kind of fud that comes about ever since the luddites with its proponents readily handwaving away the very real historical patterns following technological advancement so here s an alternate perspective firstly this doomsday scenario has already occurred we already have computer programs that write code that the vast majority of the developers it replaced can only dream of they re called compilers then we used those compilers and did it again by creating interpreters now we re well on our way to maybe pulling a trifecta with the newest generation of developer tooling in every one of those cases the industry exploded in size the thing that gets forgotten is that at every point of advancement we still have people at the lower levels maintaining and iteratively improving their tier of the technology hierarchy there are low level machine code experts working to crank out better more optimized chips there are people who are then expanding and maintaining compilers to target those new chips then there are programmers building higher level languages and jits to abstract away those compilers with every advancement we lower the barrier to enter at higher and higher levels of abstraction new programmers will adopt new languages and frameworks that are maintained and optimized by specialists in the underlying technology who themselves will be reacting to changes being made at levels closer to the machine by another set of engineers the common response then is this handwavy thing where no matter how high up in advancement you go the claim is something like well ai will do that too ignoring the time and intermediate processes that are involved in such a thing but let s say such an unnaturally skilled prescient capable economically accessible system were released tomorrow if such an entity existed i m pretty sure i can predict exactly what will happen humanity as a whole would gleefully happily and willingly go extinct why bother reproducing there s an ai that will stand in for children and will emulate being your child better than any stupid meatbag kid why bother retaining a legacy there will be an ai that will remember you more perfectly with greater fondness and reverence than any civilization ever will why have relationships there will be an ai that will replicate the desire for friendship love and companionship way better than anything as limited as a person in short if ai ever existed to the level described by doomsayers the problem becomes irrelevant to the extreme']

Name: 63_classes_computer_course_class
Representative_Docs: ['it has about software cs course the required ones are discrete math probabilities and statistics data structures and algorithms operating systems database systems computer networks software engineering programming programming computer architecture computer organization design and analysis of algorithms parallel and distributed systems compiler design computer security artificial intelligence that s beside more elective courses can be chosen from a concentration in one or more of the following computer design computer software computer networks systems and artificial intelligence that s my entire curriculum in case you are interested in knowing more', 'cs is to programming as ee is to installing electrical outlets ok that s not entirely fair basically cs typically involves the following cs intro programming cs basic data structures and algorithms more advanced programming discrete math proofs and topics like number theory set theory combinatorics etc math usually has a similar course but it s at the senior level and it s about proving stuff in calculus algorithms data structures maybe a more advanced course theory of computation turing machines etc but usually only at more theoretical cs programs so not usually taught computer architecture programming languages learning different kinds of programming styles and basics of different languages and what makes them different from say java electives for last years pick say courses from a list of possibly focusing on certain areas these include databases data science software engineering compilers operating systems cryptography ai machine learning numerical analysis and maybe or more courses depending on how big the cs program is how many profs in general you would probably only need about programming courses discrete math and algorithms data structures to get by the classic in algorithms is clrs cormer leiserson rivest and shamir the book is huge but maybe you only need about the first chapters and the rest are selected topics', 'iiuc most computer science courses require you take some variation of the following courses an introduction to programming course a discrete math course where you learn both about discrete math and writing basic proofs a data structures and algorithm course where you learn about how some foundational ds amp a work learn how to analyze them prove properties about them a systems or hardware software interface course where you learn how your computer and os works under the hood usually along with some c and assembly the goal with these courses is to give you a solid background in the core foundations of cs after that point most cs degrees seem to be structured so you can branch out and explore whatever you re interested in via electives exactly which electives are valuable will vary pretty heavily based on your personal preferences and your particular school i m personally most interested in infrastructure and other backend related things so found the following electives to be most useful to me operating systems compilers databases specifically one that focus on implementing a database as opposed to using one but there are other options of course if you re more into data science you ll probably want to focus more on machine learning and statistics courses and if you re more into frontend stuff you ll probably want to focus more on ui ux and human computer interaction etc faq how do i learn topics covered by a typical computer science degree has some suggestions on where you can find courses like the above but caveat as several other people have said a strong foundation in computer science does not necessarily translate into the skills you need to succeed in a software development job in particular one essential skill that universities have a difficult time teaching is software engineering and project management imo you can only really learn these skills when after working on complex and messy systems that you own for a prolonged period of time and it s pretty difficult to distill that down into something that can fit into a single semester some schools do try and work around this by strongly encouraging their students to try and find internships or by mandating a capstone project but this is ultimately something that i think can be learned only through experience whether or not you need strong cs skills can vary quite significantly based on what kind of job you have especially since people tend to self select into jobs that match their particular strengths this does mean that it s possible for people to end up developing a blind spot of sorts people with strong cs skills tend to believe that that having that foundation is important while people with different strengths will often disagree if your goal is just to learn for the joy of learning and self improvement i don t think this really matters but if your goal is to learn to maximize your career progression it might pay off to be more strategic make a conscious choice about whether you want to grow your existing strengths or start developing new ones and think about how that might effect the trajectory of your career']

Name: 66_aws_cloud_gcp_azure
Representative_Docs: ['i am doing a report on g nr the new mobile network infrastructure being rolled out this year and next for the lay people it increases network speeds a lot we ll have theoretical gbps speeds on mobile devices and potentially a lot higher that s great so for the sake of argument lets assume we re in a dense urban environment with great connectivity now lets assume we re google we have a phone which can take voice commands and process them into an action that requires streaming the recorded voice command from the phone gt local cell tower gt router gt larger data center gt a server processing the recording in the machine learning center ie translating the recording to text using some sort of syntax analysis to determine the request create a response send the response to the router gt local cell tower gt phone certainly seems like a lot of work so a lot of people are now talking about edge computing articles and books are talking about edge computing replacing this really heavy handed networking solution instead we ll put devices near the user so they can get a response faster eg streaming the recorded voice from the phone gt local cell tower gt edge device processing the recording on the edge device ie translating the recording to text using some sort of syntax analysis to determine the request create a response send the response to the local cell tower gt phone less hops less latency so i understand that g edge computing means we ll have less latency thanks to g and edge device locations but i don t understand the practical roll out of edge devices is google supposed to buy a server and place it in the dense urban environment how big are we talking a full server rack in my neighborhood isn t that logistically much harder to accomplish what about smaller companies how can we avoid aws or other large cpu providers not like i can deploy edge devices all over america but i can use aws to deploy my containers to their warehouses how is the g edge computing roll out supposed to work from my perspective it s not really something that small devs will be able to leverage i want to start putting my computations on the edge eg processing voice commands video content but anything that requires heavy processing either requires a beefy computer at the edge or you use a beefy computer in a data warehouse right idk i could use some help in visualizing how small businesses can leverage edge computing', 'pick a platform google cloud platform amazon web services microsoft azure there are others but these are the top that companies are using it s subjective but i m going to say gcp is the most interesting and enjoyable to work with a lot of companies are using azure and aws to run their enterprise stuff but are moving towards gcp for their machine learning ai and analytics then complete the entry level certification for your chosen platform e g google cloud associate cloud engineer some say certifications are useless but it s almost necessary now to get a recruiters attention gt worth mentioning aws is the most popular platform used followed by azure then gcp spend time just being hands on and experimenting the three platforms have a fairly reasonable free tier so it shouldn t cost you a thing to spin up some vms deploy some websites play around with networks and firewalls remember to clean up to avoid surprise bills acloudguru is a good site to do some learning and understand whats on the certification exams and they also provide you with a sandbox playground environment when doing labs if you do happen to go the gcp route qwiklabs by google is a great site to get hands on practical experience building real world things in safe sandbox environments too i m a fan of gcp if you can t tell but you can t go wrong with any of those three', 'i got into cloud computing at my first job out of college i was working with a company that was still using an on prem data center and was just beginning to look into using cloud based infrastructure from there i switched to another job at another company which was cloud specific and the entire culture was about learning a ton with the cloud and knowing how to do stuff with it that culture helped me grow into future cloud roles specialties i m a big fan of serverless and containerization right now i do a ton of work with kubernetes but before that i was big on using aws lambda and azure functions i started working heavy with the sdks and apis that cloud service providers offer and that opened up gateways to different ways to automate and create new things my favorite part of working with cloud services is that everything can truly be coded everything is disposable it s really accessible so anything i do professionally i can usually do outside of the office unless i wanted to build ai models for students don t study cloud engineering as a primary major it shouldn t be what you study in school it should be an extension of what you do professionally in school pick up the basics around programming knowing the ins and outs of operating systems network concepts all of this is more important for the cloud working with cloud services requires all that knowledge and then domain level knowledge depending on the provider majoring in cloud computing doesn t seem like any sort of reasonable thing to me']

Name: 70_chatbot_chatbots_bot_chat
Representative_Docs: ['i m new to nlp and chatbot development so i d appreciate any advice about the following i m looking to create a chatbot with the following criteria has a persona identity e g beyond just answering questions not a variation of an faq bot can vary its replies somewhat answers feel fairly natural i e if the user asks do you have children the chatbot can reply yes or i have like for the model to run in c for various reasons could i train it in another language e g python and then run in another any advice guidance would be appreciated thanks', 'good day everyone i m a college student working on a medical chatbot it will only feature some simple functions like diagnosing common illnesses cold fever flu etc answer health queries and book appointments i m not relatively new to programming as i have studied a bit of it before java php javascript but this is my first time delving into chatbots my college professor recommended that i should use dialogflow for the chatbots nlp i ve already had a framework approach in mind but my problem is that i have no idea where to start in creating a chatbot furthermore i m using mysql for its database and heroku for hosting it i was supposed to use mongodb for its database but as this project is in a collaboration with a classmate of mine i needed to use mysql as laravel does not natively support mongodb i hope you guys can lend me some of your knowledge thanks posted a similar post on r chatbots', 'chatbots are relatively new and the rise of artificial intelligence is introducing many new developments chatbots are one of the first examples where ai can be applied in practice the behavior of bots where ai is applied differs enormously from the behavior of bots where this is not applied in this article we ll tell you more about the rule based chatbot and the nlp natural language processing chatbot if you do not yet have a good idea of chatbots and conversational interfaces check out our article what is a chatbot different types of chatbots rule based bots with this type of bot communication is through pre set rules user input must conform to these pre defined rules in order to get an answer often with such bots only buttons are used since no artificial intelligence is used here an open conversation with this type of bot is not possible or very limited nlp of ai bots artificial intelligence ai is still an unclear concept for many people the idea behind this is to make robots think like humans that includes many aspects and that is why it is such a broad concept you can think of features such as logical reasoning planning and understanding languages understanding languages is especially useful when it comes to chatbots unlike the rule based bots these bots use algorithms neural networks to process natural language this is where the term nlp or natural language processing comes from the chatbot not only recognizes specific words it actually understands what someone is saying nlp chatbots learn languages in a similar way that children learn a language after having learned a number of examples they are able to make connections between questions that are asked in different ways in this way the bot understands what the question is about without being precisely programmed for it and an appropriate answer can be given in a conversation form this is also called conversational ai']

Name: 74_swe_teams_ml_infra
Representative_Docs: ['it is true my position is called machine learning engineer the name implies that it s a badass position where i create models the reality is this on the projects i am on right now i spend time data engineering setting up de resources talking to the other teams des to figure out their needs and writing scripts to do what they ask software engineering build apis and backend stuff and pipelines ci cd creating packages unit tests and such however i have been on a couple of other projects where i actually did the data cleaning feature engineering and building models both for a cv application and a time series prediction on the current projects i work with a ds but on the cv and time series one i was also the ds the definition of ml engineer is really subjective you may need a ph d a decent m s would also do to be doing mostly ds work in a decent company like faang although we work with microsoft dss and i have seen many of them with just an m s degree out of only had a ph d in our company where it s not big tech we have a few ds with a b s in stats tho who are in fact doing ds work most of the time so for an actual job in a decent company you would need a good m s in stats or cs or a ph d but if you are doing ml stuff on your own to create an app or idk build a real estate profoliio you do not need a ph d one last thing actual ml work in the industry requires subject matter expert knowledge our company specifically is shifting towards teaching ds to their smes rather than hiring dl experts', 'gt for example the work one would do at a big moderate sized tech company in a swe job would be related to web dev right it depends there are certainly a good number of web developers in the industry mind you web development in big tech often also involves stuff like running feature experiments measuring user engagement monitoring metrics etc there s also a ton of infra work which i feel sometimes gets lost in this sub in the sea of posts mentioning js css html angular react the teams that build ai platforms the teams that build distributed cloud computing tools teams that manage networks teams that manage databases etc etc tons of teams out there that aren t necessarily part of the front end back end loop of web dev gt i feel that jobs in ml are harder to come by and especially for the bigger names you need a masters or phd to really work in ml misconception in my experience at the bigger names the only thing you need to do to get an mle job is to sell yourself this might entail different things at different companies depending on their recruiting practices at companies like google amazon your recruiter will list out teams for you for team matching and you ll meet with them to determine fit you need to make sure you communicate that you want to do ml and sell yourself to the teams at a company like fb you have free reign over what teams you contact so you can choose to only search for mle positions all in all a grad degree is not required i know someone who did an internship at an analytics team in faang and is getting contacted by ml ml infra teams i myself don t have a ms phd and am an mle at faang i know plenty others that are the same of course if you re going for research positions in industry phd is more or less required gt would learning web development help me secure a better internship next summer my recruitment experiences may have been different from yours but i knew approximately web development going in hadn t written a single line of code in any web dev framework never put together a website nothing if you re aiming for the mid big tech companies this isn t what they re asking for their interviews are fortunately or unfortunately all leetcode i actually ended up as a full stack intern without knowing any webdev experience at all my background was a lot of modeling coding i could develop the statistical libraries the project needed easily but i had to learn the webdev framework since my project was basically a minimum viable product prototype', 'gt what does a swe at faang exactly do of course there are the engineers you envision product engineers that work on front facing features people that make and experiment with the day to day users see that s web development but even then they might do some interesting things you might not consider experiment with certain features analyze user metrics etc but that s just one facet of engineering at swe there are engineers that don t touch front end at all and write the back ends for applications many products have ml engineers think how does google fb know what kind of ads to suggest to you how does youtube twitter know what content is covid election etc related outside of product teams there are infra teams as you might imagine faang companies collect and compute with tons of data there are teams that develop and maintain the frameworks that other engineers use to do their computation things like ai infra how do us ml engineers do our work we use tools developed by our ai infra teams distributed computing teams etc there are engineers at fb that develop hack and google that develop golang there are engineers that specialize in networking and reliability etc there are so many possibilities at swe companies yes a lot of these require specialties if you want to be an ml engineer you ll need to know some ml obviously similarly you can t go in and work on a team that maintains the company s databases and query engines if you don t understand databases but that s up to you to choose if you just study data structures and algorithms you re a generic applicant if you get hired you ll probably be slotted in on a product team developing a front end or a back end where not a ton of specialized knowledge is required you ll learn as you go what you need to study is up to you some things you don t need to study necessarily i ve never taken a database class in my life but i learned our query languages on the job and write db queries all the time as an ml swe on the other hand this means even though i can write queries i have no idea how databases actually work i just know the qls so i can t go ahead and join one of the teams managing database infrastructure at the end of the day they re all swes they all have the same title they all have the same base pay it s up to you to decide which one you want to go for it s worth mentioning that some companies might be advertising that they re hiring for a specific engineer type maybe they specifically are hiring an ml engineer or a site reliability engineer for that listing other times you are hired generically as a swe first and then are assigned a team or even choose your own team depending on which company and how they do things based on your interests and their available headcounts i suspect that the latter is more common for new grads and the former is more common for older engineers but i could be wrong']

Name: 79_python_machine_machine learning_python widely
Representative_Docs: ['deeplabcut won t require you to learn programming at least to the degree that figuring it out would be your thesis once you get it configured took me days total with only a little bit of python experience myself deeplabcut is nearly entirely ran through a user interface handling analyzing the extracted data might require a little programming know how but again nothing that would be your defense all the hard work has already been done by the deeplabcut authors video tracking detailed behavioral scoring via machine learning will definitely be a very popular method over the next few years especially in conjunction with things like in vivo ephys or calcium imaging with your current skill set interests it seems like once you get deeplabcut running in the new lab your research questions would likely become more behavior based using deeplabcut to answer life science questions its intended usage as opposed to deeplabcut being the question itself so demonstrating you can implement this type of thing to ask interesting questions i think will be an attractive skill but it would be good to pick up some more along the way as well if those happen to be programming based and you end up really liking the computer side of things that s great but it s not so programming intensive that you will be required to become a hardcore programmer if you have any other questions let me know deeplabcut is still open source so getting it to work properly can be a little janky at first but once you re past the initial hurdle it becomes more about knowing what scientific questions you want to ask', 'since you re a computer science student i would not worry too much about the language in the us python is widely used for machine learning but the reality is most if not all of the python libraries that people use for machine learning are written using c or c native python is simply too slow for large ml tasks i recommend that you focus on the techniques and algorithms used in ai and ml it would be ideal if you could take a class on one or both of these subjects at your university given that you didn t mention such classes perhaps your department doesn t offer them here s a free set of materials for ml you ll probably want to supplement your study of ml with the book deep learning for ai i recommend finding a copy of russell and norvig s book it is the book that most people use when teaching ai courses at least in the us earlier editions are fine i would hope your school s library as a copy as a cs student i recommend that you code the algorithms that interest you there will be some techniques that might be difficult to code on your own such as support vector machines and convolutional neural networks but there are excellent implementations available naturally you can study other people s implementations but try to learn to code the algorithms yourself good luck', 'sure so ai ml will definitely want to see python there s a couple of reasons for this python for all its trouble is major support language and will integrate with almost anything from a developer perspective this great because it means it s super scalable and you won t run into an issue when wanting to say run a job within your cloud computing system python is widely used and incredibly popular from a business management perspective this means you can hire people so you ll typically find start ups or recent companies heavily rely on python because their goal is to grow python has a abundance of libraries and frameworks truthfully if it didn t have this no one would want use the language you can do almost anything with it and their seems to library that will do virtually almost anything you want easily available this makes our job as a developer easier for the reasons i mentioned above python is widely within ai ml as engineers within this realm having a language everyone understands libraries you can easily use other tools widely available it make creating say a model easier and production ready than something like r you really cannot go wrong with python when it comes to ai ml and i would highly recommend taking at lease one course within the ai domain ml nlp rl etc your professor should lean towards python for your homework assignment and if they decide to use something like matlab or r you can still take it but i d question how the professor is teaching the course with that said ml is very math based your basically applying statistical patterns to your data so a lot of it just stats amp probabilities in regards to iot it depends i feel like it will either be python or c definitely more towards c there might be some java in there as well but typically when your dealing with embedded programming or needing touch hardware the preferred language will be c lastly i am a strong believer in getting good mentorship there s only so much your courses will teach you and the bulk of your direction and influence will come from the mentors you will have in college years and in the early years of your careers perhaps in the coming academic year find a professor within ml or iot to do research with getting that practical exposure will get you to understand why these tools are used and how they are used if you haven t already look for internships co ops with a strong mentorship program edit don t feel overwhelmed or behind by this comment either like so many things in cs and in life the answer to the question will be it depends take your s as a decade to explore fail hit your against the wall a few times and hopefully really build yourself and consequently your career if ai interests you go for it if you find years down the line you actually hate it and enjoyed x more do a career switch what matters now is you trying new things failing at them growing from those failures and readapting reassessing yourself and what you d like to be doing']

Name: 82_resume_applying_jobs_wrong
Representative_Docs: ['i have years of full time experience as a software engineer and one internship on my resume i have been applying for jobs since october since october i have had calls with recruiters and technical interviews of the recruiter calls did not go anywhere one of them mentioned that they are reconsidering hiring in general for this exact position the technical interviews went well but maybe competition is so strong from more senior engineers that they can afford to get a senior engineer for an entry level salary i do get the weekly or twice a week message from third party recruiters on linkedin but i don t have the time to step away from work for a job i don t know i m applying for node js developer and angular developer at a fast growing startup is not enough information for me i am not even getting any online assessments sent back to me but maybe that is a new grad thing i have had my resume critiqued by my friends and they said that it s great i should note i used the same resume template as a new grad with a lot of success but i added my current experience on top of everything i had previously i am hoping to learn from my mistakes because i am seeing new grads with multiple job offers during a pandemic i have some theories over what is going on during the pandemic lots of senior engineers are on the market looking for their next job my job titles are a bit strange and i am applying for software and data engineering positions during my internship the exact title was technical intern also i am a machine learning engineer right now but in reality i am doing regular software engineering work alongside a machine learning data science team it might look strange for a hiring manager to receive an application for a full stack engineering role from a machine learning engineer i am too experienced for new grad jobs but not experienced enough for middle level positions as mentioned earlier i have years of experience the jobs i am applying for are too competitive i am only applying for jobs in nyc or fully remote jobs since my fiance would have an easier time getting a job in nyc', 'i would suggest looking at the classic story of abraham wald and the missing bullet holes most people are getting jobs the ones here that are posting about resumes are the ones that are doing it wrong somehow and so the important thing is to not follow their advice when they say spray and pray resumes don t have a few resumes that are tailored to the broad area that you are applying make sure that you re not sending a javascript front end focused resume to a back end position if you have a resume that is i really want to do machine learning don t send that to a non machine learning job i ll be fairly sure that you don t want this job when the suggestion is to go to indeed and linkedin don t find company pages and apply there indeed and linkedin are consistently out of date the companies don t post their jobs there and have no reason to go try to clean them up the company page is the one that has the current job openings go to and go to sub category software engineering and as of this writing there are jobs for entry level no experience required when the talk is about faang and the west coast or east coast look other places too rochester kansas city omaha detroit what about retail companies and logistics companies and fast food companies and the public sector when the talk is focused on sde look at sdet jobs too by the way many companies that aren t writing software as a product put all the people who deal with computers in a department called information technology its not all operations when the talk is about being unemployed for a year and still sending out resumes and grinding leetcode have a job get a job in helpdesk or as a sysadmin or sre or anything tech adjacent i d much rather hire a person who has a year of doing tech support or system administration than someone who has had a year of neet the things that new grads boot campers and self taught career switchers are focusing on in this sub don t reflect the reality of job searching i m not going to say it is easy but putting the dream job on a pedestal and only applying to things that match that is counter productive about that resume it is surprising to me the number of resumes that i ve reviewed for when my team has had an opening that are horrendously inconsistent i ve seen things you people wouldn t believe i ve seen javascript spelled three different ways on three lines i ve seen javascript listed twice in a single line of skills i ve seen typos that show up with red squiggles when i open it in microsoft word i ve seen attended daily standups and followed the sdlc as the first bullet point for every role i ve seen every technical term formatted as bold its really fun when this gets mixed with the javascript variations java script java script javascript i ve seen technical terms done in blue instead of bold that gave me a headache to read i ve asked questions of a person who put docker in the known skills and gotten the response back of oh i don t know anything about docker that was just something that was used at the internship', 'hello i am experienced software engineer at fang with yoe experienced is in quotes because five years isn t whole lot but i m definitely not new grad anymore i ve given dozens of interviews was a manager for couple of years in my previous company and met with hundreds of job seekers at career fairs this subreddit was great when i was looking for new jobs but it seems now more than ever saturated by either new grads or students so wanted to give some perspectives on how to get that first job everything is my personal opinion and will be geared towards people looking for entry level swe positions so you ve been applying you ve had few bites here and there but no real offer yet some people say job search is numbers game which is true to an extent but if you spend some time introspecting on what you are lacking then you can hugely improve your odds there are three main components to job search your resume people skill technical interview skill first your resume this is your foundation if the foundation is solid it will make your job searching that much easier you can tell your resume is lacking if your callback rate is low if your resume isn t the best then your focus should be improving this because it doesn t matter how good of engineer you are or master leetcoder if you never get an interview in first place to able to show them formatting and wording is important but what i usually look for when looking at resumes of new grads is whether they have internships or significant personal projects internship is usually better than personal projects but if you are having hard time getting internship or it doesn t work with your timeline then spend couple of months making personal project or better yet getting a group of friends to do a project and join hackathons this not only boosts your resume but also gives you talking points in your interviews percent of the companies will ask you to tell them about something you built now the personal skills or as i would like to call it do you know how to function in a professional setting if you pass the resume screening and online assessments first step is usually a recruiter call they are looking for red flags and whether you are really interested in their company so smile look engaged and most importantly sound excited for the opportunity do some research about the company beforehand ask meaningful questions and not something like is the lunch free lastly the technical assessment this is the most dreaded part for most people alas there isn t a golden bullet here you just have to grind grind leetcode hit the books and you will get better eventually but be sure to leetcode intentionally meaning you don t always look at solutions after being stuck couple of minutes if you had to look at a solution to solve a question then try redoing it right away or the day after you will be surprised how many questions you ve done before you can t do anymore this just means you didn t truly master the concept so take it as sign to study more lastly learn from your failures unless you are very lucky or all star new grad from stanford with internships you will fail your first interviews the company will also never tell you what the issue was so spend time on your own what you think went wrong was it technical question did you say something wrong were you too nervous debrief with your friends and mentors and pat on yourself on the back because that just means you just got that much better at interviewing think of it as getting exp points in interviewing skills also remember that luck plays a huge factor in interviews so keep trying and don t talk rejections personally learn from them and eventually you will have an offer hope my perspective will be helpful to at least some of you feel free to ask me any questions in the comments i ll answer all of them edit if you could do me a favor and ask your question as a top level comment that would make it easier for me to find you thank you edit adding a couple of caveats here i m an american so some suggestions might not be as valid in your home country also it s for someone looking for a general swe engineer role so for breaking into specific industry like ai embedded etc the process is a bit different']

Name: 85_phd_ph_getting phd_systems
Representative_Docs: ['yeah i have an engineering ph d although my research work was closer to mathematics and computer science i didn t have any specific intentions in pursuing a ph d i just liked learning a lot and a free terminal college degree seemed like a good deal i went into private industry after i finished my degree i work in machine learning and having a ph d definitely does help me with getting jobs in that area although i wouldn t say that it s a golden ticket people in my industry seem to interpret it as a general signifier of intelligence and high work ethic and people with ph ds seem to sometimes favor hiring other people who also have ph ds i ve never met someone in my line of work with a professional doctorate i suspect that people who get professional doctorates tend to gravitate towards government work or other kinds of bureaucratic institutions where the credentials are important but people don t want an interruption to their careers i do know some people who work in government and who have needed the dsc or other professional doctorate to get their positions i think doctorates of any kind are favored by federal organizations like the government accountability office and the congressional research service and maybe by others too i totally understand about wanting to keep your job a ph d is a difficult commitment and it s often driven by some form of impractical obsession i think k and years is a very good bargain for a college degree of any kind plenty of people spend more money than that on lower degrees that are less useful than it cybersecurity i would just say that you should be clear about what you re getting in exchange for that money education it s possible that the credentials will help your career in some way but the only thing you re guaranteed to get out of the degree program is the experience of participating in it', 'well there are some positions in faang and other top tech companies that are only opened up to phd graduates and the pay is extremely well too if you like working on bleeding or should i say creating bleeding edge tech then a phd helps open that door with a phd you have proven that you are an expert in that specific field of study and are able to do some serious research too cyber engineering would be pretty hard but also very rewarding as you could cover pretty much any of the defensive or harder offensive realms within the field for example let s say you chose to get your phd in computer science with a focus on security you could do research on or create new fully automated defensive and offensive security systems e g a system that automatically finds vulnerabilities on systems on it s management and dynamically patches them before a vendor has time to release a patch automated phishing email or malware discovery and mitigation better techniques to automate the stopping of web scrappers and malware disbursement through internal and external networks or new ways to conduct hypervisor exploitation and mitigation the possibilities are endless if you are really good you could get grants from the government to build systems to protect local state and federal systems or at least provide better insight for scada physical security systems symbolic execution systems cyber reasoning systems defense systems weapon systems new mapping and sensor technology life and safety systems biomedical systems unmanned aerial vehicles rockets spaceships satellites cars suvs identity systems embedded systems financial analytics and physical inventory systems e g detect fraud waste and abuse across all financial and logistics systems at the private local state and federal level ai systems that predict future changes in systems or system decay before it happens to reduce time to failure rates for physical and virtual systems the works you also open the door for later on down the road in your career for top engineering and management positions as there are some jobs that have a hard requirement on the education that cannot be bypassed by just experience e g chief engineering positions even then if you just like doing research on high risk and high reward things a phd would allow you to do that as you would be too valuable to put on operations and maintenance projects e g legacy programs at an organization company or university so your work will truly matter as you would normally be working on the forefront of technology if you choose the right job', 'ece perspective a phd is how you get to the cutting edge of technology industry rarely sits at the cutting edge because it s risky companies like a sure thing there are a few exceptions as always intel and amd are really leading the charge in silicon which means most silicon companies are pushing boundaries as well to keep up there are some ai companies doing really cutting edge stuff but most are sticking to really simple ai netflix is doing some crazy work if you like math neural tech companies tend to have a bit of the mad scientist feel but all of those require either a phd or an ms from mit berkeley or stanford or years of industry experience many of the positions strictly require a phd regardless of experience defense is nowhere near cutting edge there are lots of faculty members and national labs doing cutting edge research for the dod but once again those require phds and certainly don t have the feel of mad scientist nothing is stricter in faculty research than dod money the days of being able to hop from field to field excelling in multiple disciplines and getting noticed for your sheer genius are gone example it takes a phd just to begin understanding analog circuits and many most circuit designers design by intuition and guess and check some circuits in use in academia are very different than what gets used in industry because you can t guess and check your way to success with the more complicated ones or more often because the academic circuits are excellent for specific applications but companies want more generalized product unless it s for automotive or aerospace applications in some cases the circuits are purely academic because they work great on a bench but suck in the real world biotech is probably the most mad scientist feel but it s nothing like you re envisioning it s interesting because our knowledge of biology biochemistry and neuroscience is infantile at best so getting a phd in ee me or material science and focusing on biological applications opens interesting research but you will spend years on each project it s slow and painful and the answers are muddy']

Name: 86_phd_research_academia_phds
Representative_Docs: ['if you haven t already i d suggest taking a look through some of the recent threads on r machinelearning about research and phd programs in the field here are some examples of what i m referring to one two three i think that sub skews a bit toward industry people who are disillusioned with academia and ml research but there are definitely some legitimate points being made in those threads and others ml conferences and phd programs have grown massively in the past years or so and there have been a lot of growing pains with respect to phd admissions standards paper conference reviewing etc i m doing a phd in an ml adjacent field and using a lot of ml in my work and i m enjoying it but from what i know doing a phd in core ml ai can be a very different experience also about this but i m afraid to regret one day of not doing the phd cause i would love to do some important contribution to the ai field i think it s very possible to make important contributions without a phd and it s also very possible to spend your entire phd working on problems that are mostly of theoretical interest with no immediate applications particularly in ml if your motivation for doing research is mostly extrinsic you should make sure you re in a lab program environment that supports that otherwise you could be in for a long and unrewarding slog', 'most people studying cs want to end up as software engineers or in an analytics data science position there isn t as much interest in the academic research route as there is in say math or physics both have of grads going into academia research that said a small but formidable number of grads want to end up in machine learning research which pretty much necessitates pursuing the academia research route but there are even fewer spots in ai ml research than the already small number of folks going down the academia research path to get there also there s the issue of roi on time a lot of phd grads ultimately end up back in the same swe and ds a jobs that many are getting straight out of college or a masters because there aren t enough tenure track academic jobs or industry research jobs to absorb them all the only difference is phd grads join one level higher except most people reach that level naturally within years whereas a us phd can take up to or even years to complete so basically unless you absolutely want to go into academia research and love the idea of independent research into niche areas of cs it s probably not worthwhile pursuing a phd', 'i just got finished with grad school master s degree at uc berkeley so perhaps i am not entirely qualified to answer this but i am friends with tons of phds across various programs at cal so this is just from my perspective and should be taken with a big grain of salt getting a phd is a very hard long and rewarding journey and you should really choose to pursue it because you want to get a phd not because you just think it will pay you more it is a path that will take years sometimes up to years and can be really fun but also really really hard you mention getting a phd in ai or ml but i would say you should be looking at something way more specific than that ml ai is such a general thing and there dozens of distinct fields of research that would qualify as ai from my experience phd s usually need be doing super niche research so you re gonna want to specialize in something you find interesting there needs to be labs pis funding for it as well i know some profs are doing super cool ml related stuff like making new gans deep fake detection differentially private ml and ml heavy econometrics causal inference super underrated imo and really hard to do but if you are good at it you will always have a job those are just a very small sub sample just of things i have direct knowledge of but even just at cal there are whole labs just dedicated to computer vision self driving cars nlp classifiers genomic classifiers all of which would qualify as ai and i didn t even mention the other labs doing robotics and mechatronics which are also considered ai i hear what you are saying about how everyone is doing ml and that is true at cal as well but still the only thing that matters is how cool you find your research and how much you are able to contribute to your field so i wouldn t worry about what other people are doing just focus on you pros i will say that the pros of coming out of a top program berkeley stanford cmu with a phd are pretty great if you want to go into industry you will have amazing job opportunities as usually the coolest ml ai core data science jobs require phds and published research these roles are usually called things like research scientist or just scientist and you get to do truly cool ass shit and opportunities to work on crazy cool teams ex tensor flow microsoft research and you usually get to publish papers as part of your job if you are in a top program heck even just being a master s student the recruiting process is much different at least in some ways from everyone else for example pretty much every major company will visit your department at least they did pre covid and hold private recruiting events multiple times a year being in these programs means you will practically be guaranteed to get a first round interview with any company now how you do after that is up to you as we all know technical interviews can get really hard and especially for ml engineering they can be super hard for research scientist roles they are just as hard and you have to be able to talk about your latest conference paper the other benefits are that people you work with will go onto all sorts of companies and so you will have connections to teams everywhere and sometimes companies even fund labs directly which can be a direct pipeline for recruiting ex i know a prof who s lab is funded by fb so his students have a direct pipeline to research science gigs at fb cons there are some downsides tho the biggest issue i ve seen with phds is that it can take sooooo long years in some cases and that people burn out during this period also most people who get into these programs truly love research and academia and so they are trying to stay in academia which is incredibly hard to get into as a career it is so insane because i have seen people who are probably some of the smartest people i have ever met just fight tooth and nail for years for a k adjunct professor position when they could go to google brain and get paid k at the drop of the hat it s almost ironic in the sense that usually the people in academia that big tech wants the most are the least likely to ever go into industry other downsides include getting paid very little but living in a hcol area teaching giant undergrad courses and dealing with academia bureaucracy i know some phds who have like concurrent jobs while doing their phd ex a dude who works in core ds at fb while also doing a phd i m not entirely sure how that works out but it seems pretty rare tl dr i would say that the main point here is that getting a phd should be deliberate move because you love the research area and if you go about it just to try to get paid more you ll probably have a bad time from the sound of your post you didn t even mention research or a specific topic so i would say it s probably not the right move for you']
