'edit i feel incredibly bad by putting months in the title i shouldn t have done that it sends the wrong message and reddit doesn t allow title edit please don t take it as a generally expected timeline we are all on different paths in our life my background i am i come from a business working background and an engineering educational background i had been in sales for years before i devoted myself to programming i did some programming with c back in college but it was nothing serious and it was long gone during my time in sales it s fair to say i practically started with no foundation it has been made clear to me that my engineering background has prepared me well for this journey particularly regarding my math foundation and the ability to see things apart from a big picture i apologize for downplaying this part of my experience before this edit turning point the start deep learning python web i got interested in programming after i watched a video explaining what neural network is by grant sanderson blue brown it led me to python and deep learning frameworks i was able to follow tutorials to pick up some basic concepts soon i realized that i simply didn t have a foundation to keep going it was just too much i couldn t understand so i started learning basics with python did my first project a web project built with django framework that project introduced me to things like what front end back end database are upon until this point according to my daily log i have spent about days on deep learning about days on web development interleaved with mit introduction to algorithms and solving leetcode problems during this period the most important things i picked up were basic data structure what an algorithm is overview of a web project frontend backend database cache search etc basic deep learning concepts turning point distributed systems during my study one day my business intuition kicked in i realized that what i have learned so far didn t make much business sense i e my project and my neural network were obviously not business ready and they would never be it s not that my idea was bad it was because there was an important piece missing so i asked a question how do tech companies serve millions of requests and train their neural networks it turns out that question was the most important question in my relatively short time in programming it led me to the concept of distributed systems and the amazing course mit distributed systems it was a game changer the idea of having things modularized and distributed across many machines is what makes today s tech world possible i remember the thrill of learning all about distributed systems vividly and i was incredibly grateful that mit made open to public because the course has the most amazing labs along with the course materials implementing raft in golang i spent months on the labs everyday was a blessing surely it was not easy but passing each test made me think this is the real stuff turning point the second project soon after i completed the labs in mit i know i am getting really close to a chance to land on a real job it gave me such a confidence boost now i need one more thing a project to show potential employers what i am capable of another thing i learned is that my passion lies in backend not the frontend it s one of things you realize along the way what you really like what you don t so i devoted myself to my second project a distributed crawling system in golang from scratch i wanted to build it from scratch because that way i can experience ever detail possible frameworks are great but they shield you from low level stuff as it turns out the low level stuff is pretty important in my case in the following months the project took all my attention needless to say it was hard but also exciting and rewarding the process felt natural i didn t need a tutorial to guide me the project itself was the guide each problem leads to some google searches that in turn leads to some concepts and answers there were iterations of design i literally rewrote the whole system times and rewrote some of the modules whole more than i can remember the project was nearly finished last month and i started sending out resumes as of today i got my first offer side points leetcode and interviews i struggled with how much time i should spend on leetcode problems because many people talked about its importance currently my record is at easy medium hard total i stopped doing it midway in my study for two reasons i have grasped the basic concept i feel confident i can solve most medium level problems pretty quickly the hard ones will take longer but i won t be clueless in terms of difficulty the backend programming i am interested is more about architecture rarely is it about leetcode style problems it doesn t make much sense for me to spend too much time on it i concluded that if an interviewer really values how quickly i can solve a problem like that that s not the job i want i think many people do leetcode problems because they come up in job interviews i think it s crucial to think whether the job you want actually involves solving that kind of problem if it doesn t and the interviewer insists on it i think that s not a job yo want you want to work for a company that the interviewer knows what s really expected of you instead of jumping on the bandwagon more about job interviews almost all the interviewers i have met really liked the fact that i started my second project from scratch they appreciated my attempt to design a project and the efforts to build it although the project is in no way perfect but it showed that i can do it and they are confident that i can work on new problems on my own i think that s an important kind of mindset in programming because the field is so big almost all programmers are life long learners it s rarely about doing the same old thing over and over again it s imperative for employers to see that you can work on a new problem independently another thing that s worth mentioning is job descriptions often there are many ways to solve a problem like there are popular frameworks rd party solutions which one should you spend your time on the answer is in job descriptions find the company you want to work for or the biggest companies have the job you want see what requirements they set it will give you some clues dedication i quitted my sales job to learn programming dedication is important however breaks are expected many difficult problems were solved during a shower a walk or during workout btw i am not attached to anyone no partner no kids that made things a lot easier before i actually committed i checked my savings and made a plan i would give myself two years living on a budget based on my savings if things don t work out at the end i will go back to my old job to learn programming alongside my day job now thinking back i was able to make such a plan because if throw modesty out of window for a sec i am a relatively smart man i did well in sales to save some money i was taught how to handle money by my parents also there is the fact that i did well in college the idea of programming is never daunting to me back then it was just dull no tutorial hell i was lucky that i read a lot about tutorial hell beforehand do a project no matter how stupid the idea sounds programming languages i was also lucky that i watched some great presentations one idea that got instilled in me was that programming languages are just tools for different purposes you just have to choose the right one don t get yourself stuck with one language you d be missing a lot plus after you have learned two the third one becomes a lot easier mit open course and online classes i believe i owe mit opencourseware a lot for this incredible journey i only took two courses full mit introduction to algorithms mit distributed systems i didn t appreciate online courses a lot back then they are generally longer than a lot of amateur youtube tutorials and convey pretty much the same message but after i have spent some time on the question set and labs i knew i have found the gem yes explanations are pretty much out there on the internet many people have posted answers hardly nothing you couldn t find with search engines but the labs are a carefully designed testing environment for you to work on it guides you to solve one problem after another each more challenging than the last it is not a simple tutorial to treat lightly it is the work of many brilliant staff in one the of most prestigious schools in the world spend time on hard problems they are worth it book i would recommend only one book designing data intensive applications the big ideas behind reliable scalable and maintainable systems this book covers pretty much everything you need to know about modern applications from a big picture i have read some books along the way but mostly only as references to a particular problem this is the only one i read from cover to cover']

Name: 95_paper_authors_journal_reviewers
Representative_Docs: ['we don t think that this method has any advantage without any reason and the authors are not allowed to reply in what context does this happen in my field quite related to deep learning the authors always get to have a rebuttal unless the paper is outright rejected or it s for a conference however our conferences are not as highly regarded as in ml if i got this comment in a paper i would write a paragraph or two pointing out the vagueness of the statement and then maybe throw in a numerical comparison with other standard methods this is almost always enough to get past the reviewer and the editor usually steps in if the reviewer claims are unjustified personally i don t like the idea of reviewers being known no one in my field would ever reject papers that need to be rejected if their name was attached to the rejection most of the papers i reject are conference papers that have been thrown together at the last minute probably by a graduate student at the behest of their advisor it would be super awkward for a new student to get such a rejection with a name attached to it especially if that student then meets that reviewer in person at a conference the anxiety this would induce imo would be more detrimental to new people especially ones who don t get the guidance they need early career when they start submitting papers for the first time imagine getting a scathing or even just a politely negative review from i don t know yann lecunn or one of the other big names on your first paper it would be terrible', 'i m reviewing for a high impact medical journal a group submitted a paper where they use artificial intelligence to solve a specific technical non diagnostic task this task is perfectly suited for artificial intelligence indeed the first papers that tried it and succeeded were from in this paper the authors try the latest fad in neural network design and they conclude that this one works like the previous others with less fancy techniques what can i say it s technically correct but if it were a math journal i d be asking for many experiments to be performed so that it shows what are the differences between this solution and the state of the art ones but here it s a medical journal no one cares about math should i recommend acceptance then why not another ones that say this thing that works also works if i paint my pc green should i recommend rejection just because they bored me should i ask for further tests which ones they already demonstrated quite well that it works just not that it works any different from how it s known to do and anyway i don t want to be the asshole reviewer', 'i work in mathematics and its applications in particular in deep learning this is the first post of a series i have in mind about the review process i advocate for open review authors know who the reviewers of their papers are and they are automatically given the whole reports and also automatically the right to reply to the objections by the reviewers why if a reviewer reveals their name then they will be hesitant to say wrong things or things they are unsure the objections by the reviewers can be right or wrong don t treat them as they are gold standards however that is far from standard in the fields i have written papers on there could be review reports like we don t think that this method has any advantage without any reason and the authors are not allowed to reply also we don t know who the reviewers are and whether they are fair or competent and often there are many cries of unfairness however there have been not much improvements i know websites like openreview which is a better model the whole public knows about the content of the review they also allow authors to reply to objections the public could more easily decide who is right and who is wrong if there is dispute so this is almost what i want except we don t know who the reviewers are but one committee which has the authority could easily find out who are behind so i have a couple of questions what is the common practice of reviewing in your field i wrote what would you like it to be at least as the openreview however i like something more than that see point below how do you think it could become what you would like it could be in my case an initial thought i have is that a concerted effort from all institutes in the whole world can resolve the problem in the field in particular there should be an independent committee having the right to audit journals and conferences and so on should there be evidences of irregularities editors reviewers who show a tendency of doing bad jobs should get consequences same for journals which have the tendency of doing bad jobs papers which were unfairly treated should be reconsidered authors with solid evidences of unfairness from the journals editors reviewers should have the right to appeal to the independent committee addendum november from the responses many agree that reviewers should be anonymous to authors i have no objections with that except how to keep reviewers to be fair and hold them in account if they too often do bad jobs for or not for their own benefits the field climate environmental science seems to have a very nice reviewing environment x b also it is suggested a website where people review the review process x b looking into some of the journals there i think that the website is very informative and useful on the other hand for each journal just about a few reviews submitted and usually not as detailed as on openreview website however how can we use the information to actually improve the reviewing process x b addendum november is there any way to design a relatively fair system of reviewing some ideas i have have a standard process that decisions must follow by specific reasons give authors the right to respond to the criticism by editors reviewers don t use the reasons too technical not up to the level instead comparing with recent papers published there probably within years depending on how many papers that journal publishes a year the important issue how to have the ideas we have actually implemented this will be the topic of another post addendum december there is a comment about journals paying fees for editors referees this could help with improving the quality of peer reviewing however there are some difficulties disadvantages first if a paper is rejected then journal has no income from that paper so they cannot pay editors referees would this make editors referees to be more willing to accept papers i don t know how to resolve this issue second some editors referees may get benefits because of getting invited to review too many papers maybe there should be a limit on how many papers an editor referee is allowed to handle a year another way is that after a certain amount of fees obtained the editor referee will do things for free like what they are doing now addendum december one idea which could alleviate the fear of referees about being known to the authors is that the journals will change the way they ask the referees to do the referees will not be asked about decision on acceptance rejection or about subjective opinions like is the paper beautiful instead the referees will be asked on a definite very objective opinions for example x b is the paper on par with at least one paper published in the journal in the last years is the paper correct is the idea new original compared to the current literature is the question to be studied in the paper discussed a lot in the past current literature need more such questions addendum december one way to make the reviewing process better is to have editors serve only a limited duration say or years for a journal for their lifetime of course there are good editors who serve whole life but it can be tempting for an editor to abuse power when staying too long in a journal in particular if that journal has big weight even if the editor in question could be a very nice person they may have some hidden bias which they don t even know which affect how they choose referees and decide whether to accept a paper x b x b x b']

Name: 96_proctorio_proctoring_exam_privacy
Representative_Docs: ['i m sure there s many policies at your university that basically prevent you from being able to do anything and prove you ve already consented so no there probably isn t anything you can do i hate online proctoring and i haven t even had to deal with it in a single course the idea of it disgusts me it makes one of the most anxiety inducing parts of a course even more anxiety inducing when i went into class pre pandemic to take tests yes i was proctored by the professor surveying a room and teaching assistants walking around that feels perfectly normal to me vs installing a third party app on my device to monitor me and my web traffic and alert my professor or lock my test because the ai thinks what is potentially normal human behavior is cheating i have a task for professors who see no way around proctoring make a test that isn t from a test bank if it ends up on chegg contact them they will provide you with the email ip address and more of whoever viewed those links during the time of your test i mean it s a pandemic cheating is always bad but this is excessive there are many ways around it shorter test times unique questions or i don t know realizing we re in a fucking pandemic and just making the test open book and prioritizing true learning because god forbid people are having a hard time doing that this semester', 'i integrated integrity advocate into kajabi for a client in here s my understanding super privacy centric they are the only online proctoring solution that uses the privacy by design philosophy that i know of and they only collect the information necessary to match the test takers name to their id after the identity is verified the information is purged from their system i believe it s within minutes but it might be longer than that the same is true for the video captures they have multiple levels of tracking for my project we used level if my memory serves me that means they are basically making sure that no one is helping the test taker there are more discerning levels to protect against the opening of other browser windows etc ai captures frames from the participant s camera and flags anything that meets the criteria set by the instructor then humans review for false positives which was key for us purely ai solutions don t work constantly send false positives maybe they are better now after a human reviews all the flags the pass fail is sent back to the platform instructor and all of the data including the camera recordings are purged this is what i mean by privacy centric every other platform that i know of stores the student data thus leaving it vulnerable to a data breach and technically not gdpr compliant a quick google search will surface some of the more recent catastrophes the integration was pretty easy and i was able to deploy it on kajabi which they didn t officially support hopefully this is helpful good luck', 'again if you have valid proof of insecurities in how these companies and lms operate by all means share with the world pretending to be paranoid and pointing out no software is secure is not at all productive i m sure ceos at the relevant companies would love to add your expertise to their teams why are you arguing with a stranger on reddit over hypothetical technical jargons you should be sending your resume to these companies asap it is interesting that you wrote a long and hostile reply accusing online proctoring being insecure but provided zero concrete evidence in backing up your accusations i assume you are familiar with internet security and software development so please educate us on the specific details of how proctorio would invade your privacy beyond its designed functions anyway let s take a look at the process of taking an exam with one of these services enabled you install google chrome a browser published by google you download an extension published by proctorio from the chrome extension store and you take your exam on whatever lms your university subscribes to while taking the exam your webcam records your face and surrounding voice the extension records whatever is on your computer screen the video audio feed are monitored by ai if it detects consistent violations e g not detecting your eyes an employee of proctorio will investigate and contact you if necessary to let you know what you need to do after you take the exam your professor sees the recording and are notified of all suspicious incidents that s it proctorio does not access your local files it does not access your browsing history it does not hack your google account to look up your search history in the worst case scenario there might be a ferpa violation if someone else sees your recording of course if you decide to watch porn in the middle of the exam or take your laptop to your toilet or read out loud your social security number and your mother s maiden name or walk around with your laptop giving a tour of your living space then that s on you proctorio or whatever proctoring software cannot get those information unless you deliberately screw up']

Name: 99_quantum_quantum computing_computing_physics
Representative_Docs: ['hello so ive been really interested to take quantum computing at my university i am interested in the subject and i dont believe the course is necessarily difficult however i have career goals in data science and ik lots of popular tech companies are working in the realm of quantum computing the other thing is i would need to take two physics classes before i qualify to take quantum computing undergrad level that something to consider i have already taken two physics classes before hand as a requisite to my current major my question is is this a good idea in terms of a career in data science or should i spend more time on things more closely related to my career goals please tell me what yall think lol edit i would need an additional physics courses to take this course edit my point in mentioning that lots of tech companies are working in the realm of qc is that it would be nice to become somewhat conversationally competent about it right', 'i m invested in quantum computing research and i have to agree that a ph d is necessary for most quantum work i ve answered this question before on this subreddit and i think my advice still holds fairly well gt either you are contributing to the physical implementation of a universal quantum computer through something like trapped ions or superconducting qubits or you are working on more theoretical questions in quantum information quantum algorithms etc it does have a future but it s still too early for widespread adoption most positions require a ph d unless you want to be a specialized software developer even then there aren t many openings out there gt gt if you are looking for an immediate job position i wouldn t recommend going into this field in its current state i would only commit if you are looking to do research in this field and invest a serious amount of time and effort maybe you can luck out a bit since many startups are trying to do quantum machine learning or quantum combinatorial optimization even then you need a rather strong mathematical background edit i saw from your profile that you re taking cs at berkeley luckily that s a good place to start learning quantum from experience i took it when vazirani was teaching the course', 'the great thing about quantum computing right now is it is extremely interdisciplinary and there s a need for researchers in all sorts of stem fields i got my bachelor s in math and physics and phd in math and am currently a postdoc studying quantum optimization algorithms i work closely with another postdoc whose bachelor s was in physics and phd was technically in chemistry but he tells us no one would consider it chemistry but rather physics chemistry is a good application for quantum machines but it isn t the only one optimization finance and materials science are just some of the many others what are you passionate about would you rather focus on software or hardware quantum computing is so broad that a chemistry background isn t needed unless that s where you feel your passion lies machine learning experience will look good but it doesn t hurt to get some physics or chemistry research experience if your dream it work at google quantum ai maybe look up cvs for scientists who work there and see what degrees they have if you can t find a cv they might have an up to date linkedin profile that might have that information while i am taking the academic route with my career i had a handful of internships in undergrad and grad school all in quantum computing pm me if you want to know what programs they were specifically']

Name: 101_companies_dc_ai ml_ethically
Representative_Docs: ['i work in the ai space as applied to marine biology there s a couple of small companies non profits working in the us wildme kitware cvision cvisionai com then a couple of outreach programs at larger companies microsoft ai for earth google and government positions noaa usgs nasa mostly government contracting public or internal grants then there s a few other things outside of ai it robotics other funding tends to be on the smaller side compared with other industries self driving cars defense so less opportunities some requiring higher degrees but that depends on which subfield you want to go into', 'well i don t personally know if there s a website that has a bunch of jobs like the ones you re looking for but where i work leidos ai ml accelerator leidos is a large defense contractor in washington dc and the ai ml accelerator is its ai research division we do this type of work all the time in my case i m working on turning interpretable ml algorithms into software products what generally happens is the researchers in our division reads a few research papers and builds proof of concepts and replicates the results of the paper from there they identify a particular approach they find promising and they hand that paper over to me i read the paper and take a look at their experiments and then i build a python library that s easier to work with and then i build the supporting infrastructure to facilitate future development on that algorithm so a lot of the stuff i do is like building docker containers building ci cd pipelines to run tests running more models and datasets on through algorithm to see how it performs building the software engineering teams to maintain these projects and even creating ways to explain and pitch these algorithms to engineering teams we re currently hiring interns for summer if you re interested i can send you the application link', 'howdy i went through a very similar experience at first i wanted nothing to do with faang then only wanted to work at google and then realized that i disagreed with a lot of what those companies do additionally you ll be a tiny fish in a gargantuan ocean and i didn t want to have that experience since my internships were at large corporations i narrowed my search to small companies and was super super fortunate to join the team at ketch we make data privacy and governance software that s working towards a world where companies can only use ethically sourced data and consumers have as much control over their data as the law provides if you re interested in working somewhere with real humans doing things that will positively impact the world and be using the latest and greatest technologies working with legitimate geniuses who have tons of experience building successful software companies check us out we re also part of a startup studio called super set that focuses on companies that are building software that actually improves the world for example one of our companies builds ai that moderates online interactions helping to cut down on racism sexism abuse happening on many online platforms i m sure that there s plenty of other ethically minded really interesting startups out there that would love to have you as well don t give up keep looking for that great place for you']

Name: 102_text_image_document_images
Representative_Docs: ['if you re using raster images tiff png jpg etc standard minimum print resolution is dpi you can get away with if it s really flat colour with no text or detail top quality would be ish you need to build the image at or greater than the size it will be on the final page to preserve this resolution building it at dpi at cm wide then stretching the image to cm wide will just give you a dpi image which will look shit even if you then rescale the image it is just going to interpolate the extra pixels which will also look shit but the ideal solution is to build an import the image as a vector image svg ai pdf etc usin illustrator corel draw inkscape or similar then you can scale the image all you like assuming you are laying the poster out in a programme which handles vector images properly like indesign or one of the aforementioned vector packages if you re not sure how big your images need to be go and find a whiteboard or blackboard or a bit of wall the right size outline the size and shape of your poster and draw or stick on items the appropriate size assume no one is stepping closer than m to your poster', 'it s all relative to your level of expertise computer vision is not a problem the problem here is several fold you need to first recognize it s a document through any distortion of camera i e what if camera is not looking perfectly straight down at a document and lighting conditions and color of paper etc then you have to somehow make assumptions on how big the document is and translate the margins and spacing then you need to cut the document up into paragraphs and still recognize the line spacing while remembering sub heading font sizes and spacing and recognize the different fonts so you can tell between normal italic and other formating like underline strike through etc and somehow compensate for all that while recognizing individual letters and punctuation marks yes you can probably use a learning model to teach it how to recognize different documents with different formatting but that in itself doesn t give you a proper markeup document what you end up with is a bunch of individual letters like b x b b b b r b b a b b y b instead of b x ray b which can probably be solved with some programming logic but depends on what your model spits back out and at what accuracy frankly if you have no idea about computer vision you need to pick something a little simpler to start', 'as others have mentioned this problem could range from intermediate all the way to cutting edge research levels of difficulty the easiest option would be to use a pre created and trained library for computer vision and ideally specifically text recognition this would remove the need to have to understand the algorithms and math required to build a model yourself and it would also reduce the need to curate clean and prepare a massive training dataset it would still not be an easy task though and if you are not already a confident programmer then i would recommend picking an easier project if you want to go the hard way and build it all from scratch then you will have your work cut out lets say you want to use something pretty standard like a convolutional neural network cnn to build one of these from scratch would require a good amount of math knowledge linear algebra and calculus being the most pertinent you would also need to get to grips with the fundamental algorithms behind the design and be able to recreate them yourself in code using matrix and vector representations of weights errors etc if you want it to run efficiently as you are processing images you will also likely need to consider parallelism such as writing code for your gpu to vastly speed up all the matrix operations required for example you would write cuda code for a nvidia gpu to handle matrix and vector ops which can be optimised via parallelisation as you will be working with large datasets during training it will be almost impossible to debug at that stage so you will also need to create a testing scheme using existing datasets and known results to ensure that your network is working as intended all this done will leave you with an untrained model ready for you to actually start working if you are using a cnn or any supervised learning model you will also need a large labelled dataset in your case this would be a lot of images of text documents the input which also have the written text of what they are displaying the labels i would imagine there are already some good datasets out there so i would recommend looking somewhere like kaggle rather than trying to build one yourself ideally you would want at the very least few hundred thousand images for a decent training set less than this could work but your model is likely to fit very tightly to your training set and then not be useful in the real world you can then adjust your cnn to fit your training and hopefully testing dataset s in the perfect world you would then end up with a trained model which would be able to do what you want however this is almost never the case you would likely need to tweak hyper parameters considering expanding shrinking batching your training data and repeat the above process multiple times to get the best possible results finally there are some big issues which computer vision struggles with if all of your text documents are scans then most of these won t be a problem however if you want to use photographs of text then it s about to get difficult the two main issues you will come across are what if the document you are photographing is not parallel to the camera the answer is that text will be skewed and range in size even for the same font you could try to include more examples of this in your training set but there are a lot of permutations that would be difficult to get a model to understand whilst still maintaining an overall high level of accuracy what happens with different lighting conditions if most of your training set photos come from natural light artificial light then how well will the model identify text in darker conditions or even worse conditions where the light across the document isn t uniformly spread these are the two biggest issues and still plague modern cutting edge systems like number plate recognition models there are plenty of other caveats and difficulties in cv that you would also come across if you try to do this manually and i m sorry to say that all of this has been an oversimplification as to actually get from an image to raw text you are likely to require multiple different models each with different specialties in order to first identify text objects in the image and then secondly to extract the text so if you manage to do all of the above then you can start thinking about actually solving your initial problem of working out fonts text sizes etc and producing the markdown document you could approach this with a more standard dynamic algorithmic approach or you could go all in and building another machine learning model to attack this too either way if you manage to build all of this from scratch and get even semi decent results you could probably get your work published in a half decent scientific journal tl dr use an existing library for all your computer vision needs and really discover how hard this problem is if you fancy doing doctoral level work then build it from scratch and spend the next few months crying yourself to sleep because nothing works the way you expect it']

Name: 103_deploy_tensorflow_aws_learn
Representative_Docs: ['hey guys i m a few months into my coding journey i know python rather well and am now midway through the first course on fast ai my career goal is to be able to work with and develop artificial intelligence however i am in need of a job as soon as possible and since i know that i will not be a master in artificial intelligence i know that i will likely need to get into a similar field as i work on my skills my question is what should i learn in order to get a job in as close of a field as possible to ai ml i m pretty sure data science is not too far away from ai however i m sure the barrier to entry is close to as high as ai i was thinking about taking some courses on sql and possibly software engineering to further my resume but i am unsure if that is the correct route to take i know that many here have much more experience than i do in the field of computer science as a whole and would thus love to hear from you guys thanks in advance', 'take up ai ml artificial intelligence machine learning exploration by enrolling in udemy and free mooc courses to get basics of software programming fundamentals of statistics khan academy python sql and business management youtube and blogs vlogs are another resource i m sure you have an unlimited data package for internet access also look at becoming advanced user of excel and tableau for data science most folk forget computing ai and ml can be applied to most domains if you have a strong psychology background look at some of the challenges the current status and can be solved or improved by adopting digital solutions aka digital transformation look up linkedin or google or whatever is local best search engine for ai ml data analyst professionals and companies in your area follow them and look for internships sometime unpaid volunteer roles and entry level roles the idea is to get experience in the process this should get you some idea if you really want ai as your career it s interesting times so some of these steps may take some time so it may mean you will need to have to support yourself based on existing degree or job i read or heard somewhere ability to learn amp grow comes with the implicit unwritten directive to put in the hard work just a suggestion improve the habits to create hr a day to deep focus on this journey and slowly grow it to hours of deep focus made learning each day research other roles that need data analysis etc so that slowly you can get positions around data analysis get paid and then grow into next phase ai ml hope this helps and all the best', 'tensorflow is fine but pytorch has been dominating more lately at least in my experience learn both don t over extend yourself either have a good mastery of python and sql but be flexible with knowledge you don t need to master everything just be ready to learn new approaches become a master of googlefu know where to look or how to search for important code snippets or pieces of knowledge learn how to organize knowledge the thing is on the job it s almost always easy to apply a model once you know the business needs as long as you know how a model works or how to put together a neural network in a sensible way you ll be okay you ll almost always work on a team ideas will bounce around so being able to communicate is paramount i ve had so many of my ideas get ignored only for someone else to later repeat my idea in a better way i ve definitely learned from this having learned the hard way being able to communicate why you think a model is useful why you think a package should be used which metrics are appropriate etc will define how much of an impact you can have the thing that separates good organizations from excellent organizations is the ability to actually productionize their models this is where deployment comes in i really wish i learned how to deploy before i started my job most companies fail to deploy much at all or they deploy in very shoddy and inefficient ways however if you come in as a deployment guru you can transform an organization or be ready to take your own work to production and make effective proof of concept mock ups to drive your ideas home most companies use aws or gcp for deployment of models pick one both if you re greedy but one is fine learn how to use docker containers how to deploy those containers on aws or gcp how to build cloud based functions and how to build apis around those or with those deployments learn how to use kubernetes if you have time flask or fast api for making apis build your github portfolio with end to end hosted projects that will be sure to impress hiring teams as well other tools to consider mlflow to track models airflow to run processing pipelines what i ve experienced is Name: 107_essays_grading_writing_essay
Representative_Docs: ['if people are smart enough to use these programs as an outline and then go through and basically rewrite it if i was doing it i would just go through and copy the ai generated though', 'as others have said it may not be possible to identify all ai essays or document them as plagiarism but the essays are usually so poor that sticking to your usual grading standards will send the message to students that cheating won t get them an a or b i do a thing called showcase every week where i show off good parts of different students assigned writing as a model for the rest of the Name: 111_harvard_harvard cs_edx_cs
Representative_Docs: ['hey cs is a great course what it does really well is help you understand how to take an idea and turn it into workable code as well as introducing you to a lot of other really good concepts and ideas i highly recommend it to just about everyone starting out in software engineering it sounds like you ve got a good start on the technology c and python are both incredibly useful and flexible languages and between the two you ll have no problems picking up anything else you might want to learn the interesting thing about those languages is that they are so flexible c powers a massive amount of stuff we use every day amazon s backend spotify and python is one of the most flexible languages out there you can use it for anything from hardcore statistical analysis to machine learning my recommendation would be based on what you re interested in though are you intrigued by making a mobile app website start with some idea then start slotting in what you know into creating that idea keep in mind that c is a language that s most commonly used for doing backend processes so handling objects and doing things with those objects it s not the perfect tool for creating some sort of a gui driven application python s got libraries that let you mess about with ui objects so that might be your better bet or use the syntax you ve learned about with c and grab vscode and c net is awesome for creating interactive projects but beginning in your journey to be an engineer is tough it does seem like there s a lot to learn the best advice i can give is to concentrate on learning the concepts i can t tell you how many times understanding data structures has helped me form better models for statistical analysis and working in the terminal is something i do every day even as a manager', 'you can either start with cs edx course by harvard teaches mainly in c well structured program that is meant for people who have no prior experience in coding however the assignments are imo quite hard especially for those with no prior experience in coding personally i started with cs as well while waiting for my college to start and imo it provided me with a lot of fundamental concepts that are needed in computer science helsinki java mooc just finished this over the summer break teaches java duh from the name in a very beginner friendly way lots of different questions to practice on while you re doing the course which is always good to help you build up your logic on computational thinking later on in the course introduces one of the key paradigms in programming aka object oriented programming the odin project haven t tried this one yet but heard a lot of good reviews about it basically teaches about web development has full curriculums as well on their webpage python probably one of the more beginner friendly languages and for good reasons as well it is used in many different specializations nowadays software engineering data science ai and the list goes on and on you could do the automate the boring stuff with python course to learn more about the language and its applications', 'id want to go with the halting problem it was reading the first few chapters of roger penrose s emperors new mind on godel halting problem etc that got me into theoretical cs compared to a more applied topic it will probably be boring to most students who hate maths but who cares about them the students that matter i e the ones who might actually want to go on to study maths cs at a deeper level will find it fascinating if done properly and those are the ones you should be wanting to reach it might be a bit hard to fit into a short lecture though so really depends how much time you have you d probably need at least an hour since you would need to explain at a very simplified level what a turing machine is first the presentation in penrose s book is just so good that i d probably structure a talk around that whole brain uploading living in a simulation etc is just gimmicky philosophy i d avoid hacking is obviously cool and historically has appealed to the smarter and more technical kids but these days you d have to probably have to phrase it in a boring way here is how companies prevent credit card fraud rather than being about all the fun clever techniques that people have used to break into systems in the past how buffer overflows work etc crypto is boring i guess its politically finanically interesting but meh game of life and cellular automaton are great but its more maths than cs never a bad thing to talk about though alphafold neuralink are shit topics the details are too complex so you d need to talk about them in such a high level way that it doesnt give the smarter kids anything meaty to chew on its better to do a simpler topic properly imo other ideas basic algorithms eg generating fractals solving queens dynamic programming is supercool and can be introduced in simple ways basic machine learning techniques particularly simple models like knn that students can fully understand rather than blackboxes like tensorflow recursion in general godel escher bach has a million cool examples formal logic most students will hate it but the ones that matter should love it edit just saw you only had minutes that sucks doubt you have time to do much i d probably go with fractals game of life basic machine learning or maybe algorithms if you can frame it around problems that have intrinsically interesting solutions eg queens using backtracking and or something with dynamic programming like towers of hanoi or whatever but as someone said the most important thing is to do something youre passionate about']

Name: 113_math_algebra_calc_cs
Representative_Docs: ['they don t strictly require a cs degree but strongly prefer one you mention activision and rockstar here s activision s careers page let s look at their job postings keeping in mind many of these aren t entry level but they give insight into what employers look for says strong d math and algebra skills as they relate to game programming doesn t mention a degree though here s a rockstar job it states a degree in computer science or equivalent and strong math skills knowledge of vectors matrices and linear algebra so what does this tell us it tells us that while a degree isn t strictly necessary it s strongly preferred and cs is the most preferred degree among them all being mentioned more often than any other major it also tells us that math is an integral part of ai and of game programming in d space we work with vectors to describe where objects are and how they move matrices describe translations rotations and scaling certain types of ai require linear algebra', 'usually there s a decent amount of resources at your disposal whether it s office hours ta s or one of the various online resources like youtube or khan academy i wouldn t be afraid of math or it might lead to a self fulfilling prophecy if you decide a bachelors is right for you don t worry about what kind of math you ll have to do for cs just know that you ll cross that bridge when you come to it for example a high level undergrad intro to ai course that i took required us to have some familiarity with linear algebra and some basic multi variable calculus stuff linear algebra is not uncommon to run into in higher level cs courses multi variable calc definitely is a bit uncommon outside of ai but it s certainly possible to avoid a decent amount of math by picking certain cs courses', 'i can t really speak to why you feel intimidated but i can confirm that such a feeling is unnecessary given your stated interests traditional cs as taught in universities really is just a computation focused subset of applied often discrete mathematics it s a big topic that ranges over a lot of areas but almost all of them are sub branches that still rely heavily on an appropriate mathematics foundation albeit a foundation that can be quite specific and narrow programming language theory for instance is almost all intuitive logic and proofs while computer vision and ai tend towards linear algebra and algorithmic analysis is calculus so if you re already numerate and interested in computation you ll fit right in programming on the other hand is not really very related to mathematics though people who prefer functional programming languages would beg to differ beyond some fairly basic understanding of symbolic algebra ie this name is assigned to that expression therefore anywhere else i see this name i can read it as the evaluation of that expression and some task dependent ability to think in terms of different numerical bases and boolean logic there s relatively little mathematics involved in learning and applying programming languages to computational tasks that said having that mathematics basis once you overcome that initial intimidation and the tendency of a lot of mathematicians to think of variables as constants which trips people up early but briefly will only help you especially if you do find yourself using functional programming languages and automated proof assistants both of which are likely if you pursue university level and especially graduate level cs or ever find yourself engaged in conversation with this obsessed with formal verification hope that helps trust me though if you like math you can definitely pick up and enjoy programming']

but try and push through the struggle freecodecamp free i found freecodecamp really enjoyable and the short almost gamified lessons made it really easy to keep me motivated i was having fun i did find however that it was a little too hand holdy for me and i found myself not retaining as much information as i would have liked many of the modules i had to do or times to fully grasp them tyler mcginnis react courses now called ui dev he s a great teacher but was a little advanced for me as a beginner it s more of an intermediate course i d probably be ready to do his courses again now grad cert of data engineering anu i did this basically because it was being offered as a really cheap university short course due to covid although i did learn a lot of sql python and machine learning it was a pretty poor experience this is a whole different rant that i will spare you from it also didn t do much for me job wise as it often confused hirers as to where i wanted my career trajectory to go not the best use of months of my time the odin project free i found this to be really good the perfect balance of guiding you to where you need to be without hand holding and it allows you to develop real world skills such as using an ide git package managers etc if you re looking to get into web development this is pretty much a one stop shop to get there random other things that i found such as codewars for algorithm practice flexbox froggy automate the boring stuff and an assortment of youtube videos programming with mosh tim corey c pluralsight and other random things that i don t remember any more getting a job i feel like i made a fair few mistakes here so rather than detail exactly what i did i ll make it about what i recommend you do focus on what will move the dial in relation to getting an interview this is resume cv projects to put in your resume cv and to talk about in your interview linkedin networking meetups personal website resume optimise your resume cv this is the most important part of getting to the interview stage by far obsess over it get multiple people to review it make sure the people who are reviewing it know what they re talking about pay an expert to do it if necessary this is your number asset your entire journey comes down to someone looking at this thing for around seconds it s your one shot so make sure it s as perfect as it can be if you d like me to look at your resume send me a dm i won t go to much into what your resume should look like but make sure it s easy for them make your resume skimmable with clear sections headings subheadings bullet points if you live in the area of the job you re applying for make it clear that you live in that area and that you have valid working rights also tailor your resume to each job you apply for you don t have to change it a heap but if you re applying for a javascript job for example make sure your resume is javascript focused if you don t have much experience do some interesting projects or at least one comprehensive one and either add links from your resume or if you re short of space from your personal website and include your personal website link in your resume instead make sure these projects are easily viewable to the hirer if you re not looking for web dev jobs and you can t link to them at least provide links to screenshots of them make sure they look good as many hirers are not coders and will judge your stuff on the aesthetics other read job descriptions carefully read them or times if you need to someone most likely put a lot of effort into it and it lists exactly what the company you are applying for is looking for then you can basically parrot this job description back to them in your own words both in your cover letter and when tailoring your resume and it will sound to them like you are the exact person that they had in mind use linkedin optimise your linkedin profile the same way you do your resume just watch this youtube video from danny thompson for everything you need to know in this regard and connect with everyone relevant in your area apply for jobs through websites but also try non traditional routes if you see a job on a website try and find the contact on linkedin and send them a dm ask a simple question such as salary expectations sometimes this will get you an interview without having to go through the process whereby your resume is shoved in a pile with hundreds of others even if you don t get a reply to your message they ll probably remember your name when they re going through the pile which won t hurt get out there and talk to people find meetups to go to in your area break out of your comfort zone talk to people it turns out the people who go to these things are generally cool passionate people who just love to share their knowledge and are excited about anyone who shows passion as well i know you probably feel awkward and uncomfortable most people do do it anyway can t find a meetup in your area create one i actually started a junior developer meetup group in my area adelaide australia because there wasn t one now i know what you re thinking but i m not the type of person who organises a meetup group neither am i i was scared shitless i still am but i pushed through and did it anyway not only did it end up helping me land my first job but i m helping to grow the local tech community making friends and helping other junior developers find jobs and i conquered one of my biggest fears it feels good interview preparing for an interview again read the job description carefully before your interview think about any questions they could ask in relation to the job description and prepare answers for them in your head research the company before going in check out their website learn what they re about practice for the question of what do you know about us and why do you want to work for this company prepare something in particular about something they have done or are doing a specific feature they have implemented if you can t think of one question whether you actually want to work for them nothing impresses the pants off of someone more than saying i read your white paper on blahblah and i love your focus on blahblah prepare answers for the following tell me about yourself usually this is just an icebreaker or they haven t had time to read your resume so prepare a story about your journey and try and make it interesting give it a story arc which should be fairly easy if you re going through this journey because you re no doubt going to have struggles and conflicts what s a difficult situation you ve had at work or university if you haven t had any experience and how did you handle it what are your strengths what are your weaknesses people often struggle with this one for this question don t say things like i work too hard state an actual weakness but then talk about how you have mitigated this weakness for example i usually go with which is true i don t really like thinking of them as weaknesses i like to think of them more as challenges because when i become aware of a weakness i take steps to mitigate it for example i m naturally a pretty unorganised person so i m obsessive about my use of trello evernote and other project management tools to help keep me organised in the interview non technical part more than anything generally the interviewer just wants to find out if you d be someone that they would like to have around in the office so try to be friendly and confident but humble show that you re passionate about the work and the potential for working there keep your answers short and sweet more words don t equal better answers don t waffle keeping them succinct shows confidence end confidently try to avoid ending with and uh yeh ask at least one question at the end my go to question is pretend you hired me for this role and we re months from now what would the outcome be where you would say man i m really glad we hired standardweasel i like this question because not only does it show that you re in it to help them achieve their goals but it s also good from your perspective to see whether they ve thought about this before and whether you think the goals they have in mind are achievable remember it s a way interview you re also working out if this company is a good fit for you in the interview technical part if you don t know the answer to something don t try and make things up you ll appear unconfident and will most likely dig yourself into a hole just say you don t know but are willing and excited to learn if you have a whiteboard test talk through the problem generally they want to hear your thought process more than your answer ask for hints if you need to the interviewer usually wants to help you to get to the answer read cracking the coding interview for more help on this part this isn t my list i believe i may have ripped it off another reddit poster a long time ago but this is a checklist of things you should be prepared for if you re going for one of these types of jobs note that many jobs such as front end developer roles will not require this for example for my job i was asked a simple fizzbuzz data types basic bitwise operations string operations arrays linked lists singly linked doubly linked circular linked queues stacks heaps trees binary trees binary search trees tries self balancing trees traversing trees breadth first search bfs depth first search dfs preorder inorder postorder graphs dijkstra s algorithm a search hash maps handling collisions sorting algorithms insertion selection merge quick time complexities programming paradigm dynamic programming object oriented programming asynchronous programming functional programming other interview tips if you get nervous in interviews practice actual live interviews where you don t care about the outcome nothing makes you better at interviews than doing a ton of them if you re not getting that many interviews apply for mcdonalds or anywhere else that will give you an interview anything to get practice if you get the job you can always say no the more interview you do the more confident you ll get it s the same as dating and just like dating you should try to avoid one itis think of every interview as just a practice for the real thing opportunities and interviews are like buses there s always another one around the corner don t take the interview process personally i m going to repeat this one because it s really important and really hard to do don t take the interview process personally you re going to get rejected you re going to get ghosted it happens to everyone this isn t a reflection on you maybe they already had somebody in mind for the job and were required to interview due to regulations maybe they were looking for someone with less experience maybe they thought you were too smart and thought they d steal your job maybe they hired their son maybe you just look like someone who stole their girlfriend boyfriend in high school just move on other don t let age stop you from my experience i didn t find there to be any age discrimination nobody looked twice at the fact that i am in fact the most common response was that s awesome if anything it was an advantage as it showed that i was passionate determined and had soft skills that younger people wouldn t have gained yet my theory is that the age discrimination myth comes from years ago when the only people who were technological natives were young people and therefore you didn t really see many older people in tech whereas now anyone under has pretty much grown up with technology in some form don t rush you re probably going to want to churn through lessons and youtube videos because you want to learn quicker this only slows down the process make sure you understand what concept you are trying to learn and then make sure you fully understand it before moving on to the next thing test yourself by trying to build something using the concept you re learning your brain is really good at tricking you into thinking you know something there s a big difference between being able to follow along and understand something someone is doing on youtube compared to being able to actually implement it yourself it s hard to realise how much you re learning kind of like how you don t realise a person that you see everyday is ageing until you look back at old photos after months i often feel like i haven t learnt much until i remember when i started i didn t even know what a javascript was so try and remember where you came from and trust the process you re going to feel like quitting you re going to have moments where you spent hours debugging code only to realise you forgot a semi colon you re going to feel like an idiot you re not stupid']

Name: 120_ai_ai ml_ml_duplicated
Representative_Docs: ['imo we are about to see a huge shakedown in the ai business there has been a ton of hype but the viable products out there does not lead to the hype in many cases it is almost a religious argument over what is conciseness and what is intelligence is it a physical phenomenon that can be duplicated in electronic and software or is it beyond the physical from that a lot of researchers are in denial over what can be done with inductive and regressive programming called ai as far as one entering the field i d say not to take too much of it seriously and avoid those with near dogma philosophies saying how the mind works and you must obey them because of their degrees and published works play around do strange stuff have fun eventually these ai algorithms will hit consumer level that will lead to a fragmentation of the current industry there is a lot of opportunities out there when all these big domains are duplicated ten fold all over the world', 'i have an advanced degree in ai what we can do with it is amazing but it doesn t generalize across problem domains the idea that it s ai driving a car and not a whole host of subsystems that utilize ai is also a huge gap in the public s understanding the ai itself in self driving cars is a diverse echo system that has an orchestration layer the problem is when people hear ai they think oh it s easy no ai problems require a ton of code to utilize the results the ai doesn t drive the car it makes predictions on actions to take', 'lots of student often overestimate what ai ml is capable of without understanding when to and when to not use it i imagine ml ai will eventually be part of the core curriculum rather than an elective that focuses on the usage and limitations of ml ai for example similar to how we learn about basic ds maps lists arrays graphs etc i imagine this is the route ai ml will take in the future of course there will still be researchers and scholars who will continue to push the boundaries of what we can achieve but ai ml will definitely become more accessible for the average engineer even more than it is today going ai is not the ultimate choice build massive learning systems and infrastructure still requires many engineers who are good at different non ai ml things for example if you need multiple servers that collect filter and sanitize data who is going to build that if you are an ai ml engineer who has never learned to create scalable and large scale systems then your ai ml knowledge is useless from a personal anecdote the number of recent grads who only want to work on ml ai insane and imo quite foolish being a good engineer is about understanding the problem you re trying to solve and finding the best solution to that problem you can t go into a construction zone only wielding a hammer you need to bring your entire toolset and use what is the best tool for the job']

Name: 122_ml_saying ai_shows_shit
Representative_Docs: ['you are doing the thing you are suggesting against you re a data scientist the ways you use ml are not the only ways it is used you saying ai is the same as ml shows you re level of knowledge in the area and you saying ai only needs probability and statistics just shows you didn t even bother to google the subject you re trying to talk about honestly no hard feelings but don t gatekeep stuff on the internet it just makes the conversation feel like ego stroking edit now it hit me you re self taught and feeling the need to defend your ways so you have no idea what s it like needing to implement stuff on your own have a good day fella', 'ml is already largely stagnating as the scope of problems it is able to solve to satisfaction is fairly limited it s extremely powerful in robust and plentiful datasets like the mri scans where it is able to detect tumors well before a human can these solutions resulted in enormous profits and this in turn led to people being overly optimistic about how useful ml is in the rest of the world the problem with ml is that as soon as you attempt to solve a problem involving human behaviour and i mean in the broadest possible sense then everything goes to shit real fast an insurance company is able to take its huge datasets and optimise for premiums and without guesswork give the fairest offer to their clients until the client wants to know why their premium is the way it is then the insurance company literally has no possible way to answer this question because to them the ml algorithm that delivered this result is incomprehensible it s a black box that can t be interpreted do jews get a higher premium due to an increased fire hazard or is the algorithm mainly optimising for people who happen to light a lot of candles in their house if the company can t prove what caused the increased premiums for this jewish person then they re now liable to discrimination lawsuits ml sucks at answering why questions and yet why questions are what drives business decision making executives want to know what causes particular results in their company and ml has been overpromising in this regard in order to answer why questions a model needs to be able to compare its results against fictional data where a different decision was made for this a machine learning model needs to be able to render simulations a model needs to not just be a big stack of node layers but nodes need to be clearly identified and categorised and in this regard data science is in its utter infancy we re on fresh grounds here there s an enormous amount of methods left to explore however your employers might not be interested in this they want quick results it might still take a while before causal machine learning models enter into the real world but when they do the value these will provide will be jaw dropping and finally how is this all relevant to a software developer looking to get into data science by now the possible dead ends in your hypothetical career should be more clear by now you can go real deep into machine learning become a kaggle grandmaster with accuracy leaderboard topping models and still be utterly unemployable because these skills don t necessarily transfer into the real world machine learning still needs a couple of new breakthroughs before it is what the mainstream believes it is and until that happens all the employers currently looking for ml experts may realise that a lot of it is hype', 'gt you are doing the thing you are suggesting against i have industry experience so you judging the gamut of machine learning built quite a bit on my own implemented architectures from papers and can promise you that outside of academic research and a few large companies you can do fine without a deep knowledge of linear algebra when you get to the point of needing it you are doing work that typically requires a phd gt you saying ai is the same as ml shows you re level of knowledge in the Name: 123_matlab_python_toolbox_libraries
Representative_Docs: ['the problem is that there are just not that many resources for doing ml in matlab compared to python with the top tools tensorflow and pytorch being python based there is just enormously more resources and examples out there just google python machine learning vs matlab machine learning python has about times more hits tensorflow alone has more hits than matlab in total for object recognition or image recognition there are more than twice as many python hits as matlab ones you also don t need a license to use ml with python which means there is a ton more examples and pre programmed code to work off of for example try object recognition at github there are nearly ten times more python projects than matlab ones matlab is great for a lot of things but it just hasn t taken off in the machine learning domain to the extent python has', 'yes but it also works the same way against matlab hence my example of umap what is the matlab equivalent for huggingface plugins on spacy gw bethe salpeter equation solution approximations even reasonably basic tools such as faiss are very difficult to consider in such an environment these things are trivial in other languages there are some tools that are approximately equivalent such as radiation oncology tools however matlab costs thousands upon thousands of dollars for each individual extra package on top of the general matlab license pytorch vs matlab neural network tooling is miles apart and is precisely why everyone does ml with python don t get me wrong theres still some use cases for it but they re pretty hard to justify with the cost if it were free it would be great theres a lot of very smart people working on packages in other languages which would require an immense effort to implement in matlab however this places us directly back at the beginning point which is that the wonderful functions that you mention in matlab cost thousands upon thousands of dollars but in other languages they re generally free after working with matlab extensively in industry and then moving towards academia with open source languages it is typically quite incredible how much foss development excels at a much faster rate', 'python is used a lot in industry both due to matlab s extremely high licensing costs licensing issues deployment complications etc it isn t used universally in industry though since matlab has some benefits in particular fields it really depends on what you do so knowing both will be a big benefit they can do a lot of the same things and have roughly comparable performance on average but each is slightly better at certain things matlab is better at pure linear algebra simple graphing calculator type stuff if it happens to have a function that does exactly what you want that python lacks or if you need simulink python is better if your program is more than a couple of functions if your dataset is bigger more complicated messier or higher dimensional if you want to do machine learning or computer vision if you need to interact with other hardware software or web resources of you need to make your own data structures or if you need more interactive plots more complicated plots fine control of your plots or more unusual plots types in personally use python whenever i have a choice since in most real world situations where things get remotely messy or complicated python tends to win out in fields i work in one big difference is philosophical and may take some getting used to python is designed to encourage good programming practices this means in practice you need to be a bit more explicit about what you want to do python is less likely to try to guess when something is ambiguous is less likely to mislead you about what is going on and overall it is generally clearer what a python program wi do than a corresponding matlab program due to the much fewer corner cases and less inconsistent behavior a big problem i see is trying to write matlab code in python you can do it but it will be longer uglier and slower than if you took advantage of what python offers a lot of annoyance i see from matlab users trying to switch to python is from them trying to force it to do things the way matlab does them when really the matlab way of doing things is often just a work around for flaws in the matlab language and python offers much better ways to do it so you can jump into python and start programming useable programs in a few hours but it will likely take longer to learn how to actually let python help you']

Name: 126_astronomy_chemistry_astrophysics_experts
Representative_Docs: ['i m an experimental materials scientist with expertise in electron microscopy while i still do some experiments i m now mostly working on data related projects developing ways to extract more quantitative information from our images if you want to transition to the computational side i think finding something analogous is your best bet your experimental expertise would still be very valuable because you understand the limitations of the techniques and what information can be extracted from the data i ve found that this knowledge is severely lacking in my colleagues with a purely computational background to get into such a position you mainly need to invest some time into learning python and maybe a bit of machine learning to go into the pure theory computational field i think it may be too late to catch up being able to do a bit of python is necessary but insufficient python is just a tool and does not constitute domain knowledge acquiring relevant domain knowledge requires substantial study of theory algorithms and computational topics you also need to specialize at least in materials science there s dft experts md experts fem experts phase field experts computational thermodynamics experts machine learning experts just like you are probably an expert by experience on specific techniques and chemical materials systems so they have expertise in their computational tools you may be able to run an md or dft simulation with a bit of instruction but properly interpreting the results and iterating is what requires the years of experience', 'i m
